topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
The emergence of in-context learning (ICL) capabilities in large language models follows a distinct scaling law primarily governed by model depth rather than total parameter count or training data size.

BACKGROUND:  
While existing scaling laws emphasize parameter count and dataset size as principal drivers of emergent capabilities in LLMs, the specific architectural factor of depth remains under-explored in relation to ICL. Understanding whether increased depth uniquely enhances ICL is crucial for more efficient model design, as current paradigms assume that larger parameter budgets or more data alone yield better few-shot generalization. This research addresses the open problem of isolating depth’s causal role in ICL emergence from confounding factors like width and training scale.

APPROACH:  
We propose to train transformer-based language models with identical parameter counts and fixed pretraining data, systematically varying model depth while compensating with width to maintain parameter budget parity. Architectural microparameters such as attention head counts and feedforward layer ratios will be rigorously controlled to isolate depth effects. The hypothesis predicts that deeper, narrower models will exhibit superior in-context learning performance compared to shallower, wider counterparts, independent of total parameter count or training data size.

EXPERIMENT:  
Models will be pretrained on the Pile dataset (Gao et al., 2020) with three architectural variants—deep-narrow, baseline, and shallow-wide—each at roughly 1.5 billion parameters. Evaluation will be conducted via few-shot accuracy and perplexity on LAMBADA (Paperno et al., 2016) and select BIG-bench tasks (Srivastava et al., 2022) emphasizing in-context reasoning such as logical deduction and multi-step arithmetic. Statistical significance will be established through multiple independent runs and bootstrap resampling, with a falsification criterion defined as shallow or wide models matching or exceeding deep models within a 1% absolute accuracy margin and overlapping confidence intervals.

NOVELTY:  
This study uniquely isolates depth as an independent scaling axis for in-context learning emergence by tightly controlling parameter count, data size, and architectural microstructure—surpassing prior scaling laws that conflate these factors. It offers a formal experimental and theoretical framework to rigorously test the causal influence of depth, providing actionable insights for LLM architecture design beyond existing parameter- or data-centric scaling paradigms.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate whether empirical scaling laws for large language model (LLM) performance exhibit distinct phase transitions—manifested as temporary performance degradations at specific model sizes—by training a densely sampled suite of transformer models from 10M to 10B parameters and rigorously analyzing performance curvature metrics across multiple downstream tasks.

BACKGROUND:  
While prior research (Kaplan et al., 2020; Scaling Laws for Downstream Task Performance, 2024) generally reports smooth, monotonic improvements in LLM performance with increasing scale, anecdotal evidence and some theoretical arguments suggest there may be intrinsic phase transitions at certain critical model sizes where performance temporarily dips before improving. Understanding whether such phase transitions are fundamental scaling phenomena or artifacts of training instability, dataset choice, or architectural variation remains an open problem critical for optimizing model design and training efficiency.

APPROACH:  
We propose to systematically train a finely-granular series of decoder-only transformer LLMs spanning 15 carefully chosen model sizes densely covering the 10M to 10B parameter range, with particular focus on suspected critical scales near 100M, 1B, and 5B parameters. Each size will be trained with three independent replicates using uniform hyperparameters and 300B tokens of the Pile dataset to control for training variability. Performance across diverse evaluation tasks—LAMBADA (perplexity), WinoGrande (accuracy), and Hellaswag (accuracy)—will be measured at frequent checkpoints and smoothed with Gaussian kernels. We will quantify phase transitions using the statistically bootstrapped second derivative of task performance with respect to log(model size), controlling for confounds such as depth vs. width scaling and training schedule variations via ablations.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train transformer models with fixed architecture variations to reach target sizes, evaluate on LAMBADA, WinoGrande, and Hellaswag using perplexity and accuracy metrics, and detect phase transitions by testing for significant negative curvature (second derivative) in performance vs. log(model size) curves with bootstrapped confidence intervals. Ablations on training schedules and synthetic benchmark tasks will isolate intrinsic scaling effects from training artifacts.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates dense model size sampling, multiple replicates, rigorous statistical curvature metrics, and extensive ablations to directly test for intrinsic phase transitions in LLM scaling laws—going beyond prior studies that assume smooth power-law improvements—thereby clarifying whether observed non-monotonicities are fundamental phenomena or training artifacts.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate whether scaling laws for large language models exhibit a fundamental shift beyond one trillion parameters, manifesting as diminishing returns in zero-shot reasoning accuracy measured by performance plateaus on rigorous multi-domain benchmarks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs suggest steady performance improvements with increasing parameter counts, but it remains an open question whether this trend persists indefinitely at extreme scales. Understanding if and when these scaling laws break down—particularly beyond the trillion-parameter threshold—is critical for guiding future model design and resource allocation, as training models beyond this scale demands enormous compute and data investment.

APPROACH:  
We propose to systematically evaluate zero-shot reasoning performance of LLMs ranging from 300 billion to 2 trillion parameters, controlling for training data volume, distribution, and compute to isolate the effect of model size. Using consistent zero-shot prompting without few-shot exemplars, we will measure exact-match accuracy on carefully selected benchmarks that rigorously test logical, mathematical, and commonsense reasoning, namely ARC Challenge, GSM8K, and BIG-bench Hard subsets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate models at approximately 300B, 700B, 1T, 1.5T, and 2T parameters, measuring zero-shot accuracy gains per 100B parameter increments. Diminishing returns are defined as less than 0.5% absolute accuracy improvement per additional 100B parameters beyond 1T on at least two of the three benchmarks, confirmed via multiple runs, bootstrap confidence intervals, and prompt calibration to ensure statistical robustness.

NOVELTY:  
This work introduces a theoretically motivated threshold analysis of scaling laws at the trillion-parameter scale, implementing rigorous experimental controls to isolate parameter count effects and employing sensitive multi-benchmark zero-shot reasoning metrics. Unlike prior empirical scaling studies, it explicitly tests a quantitative diminishing returns criterion grounded in theoretical considerations and comprehensive uncertainty quantification, providing actionable insights for the next generation of LLM scaling.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate whether the sample efficiency of large language models (LLMs) continues to scale linearly beyond 1 trillion parameters or plateaus due to emergent internal feature redundancy, by rigorously measuring loss reduction and zero-shot task gains across an expanded range of ultra-large model sizes with controlled training dynamics.

BACKGROUND:  
While scaling laws for LLMs have shown consistent improvements in sample efficiency up to hundreds of billions of parameters, it remains an open question whether these gains plateau at extreme scales due to internal feature redundancy or other architectural constraints. Clarifying if and when efficiency saturates is critical for guiding future resource allocation and model design at trillion-parameter and beyond regimes.

APPROACH:  
We will train transformer-based LLMs at 100B, 300B, 600B, 1T, and 1.5T parameters using a unified training recipe with tuned hyperparameters per scale to isolate pure scaling effects. Sample efficiency will be quantified as the cross-entropy loss reduction per billion tokens on a held-out C4 validation set, complemented by zero-shot accuracy improvements on OpenWebText2 and the MMLU benchmark to assess downstream generalization and reasoning.

EXPERIMENT:  
Models will be pretrained on the C4 dataset and periodically evaluated for cross-entropy loss and zero-shot task performance; efficiency scaling curves will be fitted with power-law models including confidence intervals to detect plateaus or continued gains beyond 1T parameters. Baselines include Kaplan et al. (2020) scaling curves up to 175B and 600B GPT-3–style models trained with comparable hyperparameters.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work limited to sub-trillion scales or theoretical extrapolations, this study empirically probes ultra-large model regimes with carefully controlled training and evaluation protocols, introducing direct metrics of sample efficiency and rigorous statistical criteria to test the feature redundancy plateau hypothesis, thus providing definitive empirical evidence on scaling constraints at trillion-parameter scales and beyond.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
The scaling efficiency of large language models for rare-word prediction exhibits diminishing returns beyond a model size threshold that depends on training data diversity and architecture, but targeted rare-word data augmentation and adaptive architectural mechanisms such as sparse attention and retrieval augmentation can yield sustained rare-word accuracy improvements at large scales.

BACKGROUND:  
While existing scaling laws demonstrate broad performance gains with increasing LLM size, rare-word prediction remains a persistent challenge due to the long-tail lexical distribution and limited low-frequency token exposure. Prior work focuses on overall perplexity or general downstream accuracy without isolating rare-word behavior or systematically investigating how data diversity and architectural biases modulate scaling efficiency for rare lexical items. Understanding these interactions is critical to pushing rare-word modeling beyond current limitations.

APPROACH:  
We propose a factorial experimental design training transformer decoder-only LLMs at three scales (125M, 1.3B, 6B parameters) on two curated data regimes: (1) WikiText-103 baseline and (2) WikiText-103 augmented with targeted rare-word-enriched corpora drawn from scientific abstracts (S2ORC) and social media (The Pile). Architecturally, models include standard GPT-like and variants augmented with sparse attention and a retrieval-augmented generation mechanism to explicitly incorporate rare-word contexts. We rigorously construct fixed, held-out rare-word evaluation benchmarks from OpenWebText’s bottom 1% token frequency and the RareWords dataset to disentangle memorization from generalization.

EXPERIMENT:  
We measure rare-word next-token prediction accuracy (top-1 and top-5), rare-word token perplexity, and semantic contextual accuracy via masked word prediction and rare-word paraphrase consistency, alongside overall perplexity to ensure general language modeling quality. We test for scaling saturation of rare-word accuracy on baseline data, and evaluate whether data augmentation or architectural innovations yield statistically significant (>2% absolute) improvements at 6B scale without overall perplexity degradation, confirming or falsifying the diminishing returns hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates theoretical motivation linking rare-word prediction saturation to lexical distribution and model inductive biases, employs a controlled factorial design isolating scale, data diversity, and architecture effects, and pioneers combining sparsity-based and retrieval-augmented architectures tailored for rare-word modeling—advancing beyond prior scaling law studies that overlook rare-word-specific phenomena and targeted interventions.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Applying an adaptive, recurrent neural network-based real-time syndrome decoder that dynamically incorporates device-specific, time-varying noise profiles reduces logical error rates in distance-3 surface codes on NISQ devices by at least 30% compared to static decoding methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Current quantum error correction (QEC) approaches for NISQ-era devices often rely on static decoders that assume fixed or slowly varying noise models, limiting their effectiveness given the non-stationary and device-specific noise characteristics present in real hardware. This results in suboptimal logical error rates that impede practical QEC deployment. Developing adaptive decoding strategies that respond to real-time noise fluctuations remains an open challenge critical for improving QEC performance without prohibitive overhead.

APPROACH:  
We propose a low-latency adaptive decoder architecture based on a recurrent neural network (RNN) that continuously updates its decoding parameters each QEC cycle using real-time syndrome measurements combined with dynamically extracted noise characteristics from device calibration logs. This adaptive decoder will be benchmarked against two static baselines: (1) a minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM) decoder assuming fixed noise parameters, and (2) a static neural network decoder trained offline on historical noise data without adaptive updates. The system architecture explicitly addresses real-time feasibility by integrating fast noise parameter inference and decoder update pipelines compatible with NISQ timing constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate logical error rates on distance-3 surface codes using syndrome and noise profile datasets from IBM’s 27-qubit Falcon processors and QSCOUT trapped-ion simulators with controlled amplitude damping and dephasing noise channels. Logical error rates will be measured over at least 1,000 QEC cycles per trial under matched noise conditions. Success is defined by achieving a statistically significant (p < 0.05, paired t-test) reduction of at least 30% in logical error rate compared to both static baselines; failure to meet a 15% reduction will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work relying on static or offline-trained decoders, this approach uniquely integrates recurrent neural networks for real-time adaptive decoding informed by continuously updated, device-specific noise characterizations within the stringent timing constraints of NISQ QEC cycles. The combination of adaptive machine learning decoding with rigorous experimental validation on calibrated multi-qubit datasets provides a novel framework that directly addresses the dynamic noise challenges in near-term quantum hardware.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implement adaptive, real-time feedback loops within low-overhead quantum error correction codes on IBM Falcon NISQ devices to reduce logical error rates by at least 20% compared to static QEC protocols under identical noise conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current low-overhead QEC codes, such as those developed in "Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices" (2026), achieve modest logical error rates (~0.0065) but rely on static recovery procedures that do not leverage real-time syndrome data. The limited qubit resources and short coherence times of NISQ devices constrain error correction efficacy, and existing methods lack integrated adaptive feedback mechanisms capable of dynamically tailoring error recovery within a single QEC cycle to mitigate evolving noise and hardware drifts.

APPROACH: We propose embedding real-time adaptive feedback loops into the existing low-overhead QEC code framework by utilizing IBM Qiskit’s dynamic circuit and pulse-level controls, enabling conditional gates and adaptive decoding triggered by syndrome measurements during the quantum circuit runtime. This approach includes detailed latency modeling and benchmarked classical processing optimizations to ensure feedback turnaround times fit within qubit coherence windows, as well as integrated noise calibration and measurement error mitigation protocols to maintain fidelity and accuracy of syndrome extraction and recovery operations.

EXPERIMENT: Logical error rates will be measured on IBM Falcon processors (ibmq_casablanca, ibmq_mumbai) and corresponding Qiskit Aer noise models derived from their characterized noise profiles by performing ≥10,000 shots per circuit for both static and adaptive QEC implementations, with statistical testing to confirm at least a 20% reduction in logical error rate. Control experiments involving interleaved randomized benchmarking and noise tomography will validate noise stationarity and measurement fidelity throughout the trials to ensure fair comparisons.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that applies static QEC or post-execution decoding, this study pioneers the integration of low-overhead QEC with fully hardware-implemented, real-time adaptive feedback loops within single QEC cycles on publicly accessible NISQ devices, supported by rigorous latency analysis and error mitigation—thus providing a practically viable path toward significant logical error suppression under realistic hardware constraints.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implementing an adaptive quantum error correction (AQEC) scheme that dynamically tunes syndrome extraction frequency and decoding thresholds based on real-time noise characterization from IBM Falcon and Eagle processor datasets will reduce logical error rates in NISQ devices by at least 20% compared to static low-overhead codes under matched hardware noise conditions.

BACKGROUND: Quantum error correction remains a critical bottleneck for practical quantum computing on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices, as existing static QEC codes either incur prohibitive overhead or fail to fully exploit temporal noise variations. Current state-of-the-art static low-overhead codes achieve modest logical error rates (~0.0065), but do not adapt to dynamic noise fluctuations inherent in NISQ hardware, limiting their effectiveness and scalability.

APPROACH: We propose an adaptive QEC framework that leverages interleaved calibration circuits and real-time tomography on IBM Falcon and Eagle noise datasets to characterize noise parameters with sub-second latency. Using a lightweight reinforcement learning agent constrained by realistic hardware adaptation capabilities (fixing code distance per run and dynamically adjusting syndrome extraction cadence and decoding thresholds), the system continuously tunes encoding parameters in a closed feedback loop to minimize logical error rates during QASMBench benchmarking circuits.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark averaged logical error rates across multiple independent noise snapshots from IBM’s publicly available noise profiles, comparing the adaptive scheme against two baselines: (1) the low-overhead QEC code from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices” (2026) and (2) a canonical static surface code optimized offline for the same noise conditions. Statistical rigor will be ensured via repeated runs, interleaved adaptation on/off cycles, and hypothesis testing with p-value thresholds < 0.05; success requires ≥20% logical error rate reduction relative to the best static baseline.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats QEC codes as static with offline optimization, this approach integrates real-time noise characterization and reinforcement learning-driven parameter tuning within hardware-imposed constraints, providing a concrete methodology and empirical validation framework for adaptive QEC on publicly characterized NISQ devices—a previously unexplored middle ground between static codes and fully dynamic, hardware-infeasible schemes.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implementing adaptive, real-time classical feedback control during quantum error correction cycles on IBM Falcon NISQ devices reduces logical error rates by at least 20% compared to static QEC decoding under identical noise conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) methods in NISQ devices face challenges due to limited qubit counts, non-stationary noise, and tight coherence budgets, limiting their practical efficacy. Static decoders with fixed syndrome-to-correction mappings cannot dynamically respond to evolving noise patterns, leading to suboptimal logical error rates. Demonstrating a scalable real-time adaptive feedback mechanism promises to enhance QEC performance within the constraints of existing hardware.

APPROACH: We integrate a low-latency classical controller—implemented on FPGA or GPU hardware closely coupled with IBM Quantum Falcon r4T processors—that performs Bayesian inference combined with machine learning-based decoding using device-specific calibration data. This controller adaptively updates error estimates and selects corrections after each syndrome measurement round within the qubit coherence window, replacing static decoders like minimum-weight perfect matching (MWPM) for surface codes and fixed lookup tables for the [[5,1,3]] code.

EXPERIMENT: Using publicly available IBM Quantum Experience datasets of 10,000 repeated QEC cycles on Falcon processors running the [[5,1,3]] code and surface codes, we benchmark logical error rates with and without adaptive feedback under matched noise and qubit mappings. Rigorous noise stationarity verification and interleaved randomized benchmarking ensure controlled comparisons. The key metric is logical error rate per QEC cycle; a ≥20% reduction with adaptive feedback over static decoding confirms the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously demonstrates, for the first time on real NISQ hardware, the feasibility of executing adaptive feedback decoding within coherence time constraints by providing a detailed latency budget and hardware integration strategy. It advances beyond prior static or offline adaptive decoding schemes by combining Bayesian inference and machine learning in real-time closed-loop control tailored to fluctuating noise patterns, validated through comprehensive experimental controls and noise characterization.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Implementing adaptive, real-time feedback control based on syndrome measurements in NISQ devices can reduce logical error rates of a [[5,1,3]] low-overhead quantum error correction code by at least 30% compared to its static counterpart under identical noise models and hardware conditions.

BACKGROUND: Current NISQ devices face significant challenges from noise and limited qubit counts, restricting the efficacy of traditional quantum error correction schemes that require high overhead. While low-overhead QEC codes mitigate qubit cost, their static decoding limits error suppression potential. There is an open question whether integrating adaptive, real-time syndrome-based feedback can meaningfully improve logical error rates within NISQ hardware constraints without incurring prohibitive overhead or measurement back-action penalties.

APPROACH: We propose to augment the [[5,1,3]] low-overhead QEC code (as introduced in “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices,” 2026) with an adaptive feedback mechanism that processes syndrome measurement outcomes during circuit execution to conditionally adjust corrective operations and gate scheduling. The feedback loop will be implemented using Qiskit Pulse and OpenQASM 3.0 conditional instructions on IBM Quantum Falcon processors (ibmq_mumbai, ibmq_toronto) with realistic noise models capturing amplitude-damping, phase-damping, depolarizing noise, and calibrated gate errors. Control experiments will isolate measurement back-action and overhead effects, and offline adaptive decoding will be used as a near-term alternative where real-time control latency is prohibitive.

EXPERIMENT: Logical error rates will be benchmarked over ≥10,000 runs per configuration, comparing the static [[5,1,3]] QEC code baseline against the adaptive feedback-enhanced version under identical pulse-level calibrations and noise profiles available through Qiskit’s noise model datasets. A statistically significant logical error rate reduction of ≥30% (e.g., from 0.065 to ≤0.045) will validate the hypothesis; overlapping 95% confidence intervals or degraded performance will falsify it. Noise stability will be ensured through interleaved randomized benchmarking and calibration tracking before, during, and after experiments.

NOVELTY: This work explicitly integrates and experimentally benchmarks real-time syndrome-based adaptive feedback within a low-overhead [[5,1,3]] QEC framework on commercial NISQ hardware, addressing practical constraints in latency, measurement back-action, and noise stationarity. It advances beyond prior static or offline adaptive QEC schemes by proposing a concrete co-processing architecture and rigorous falsification criteria, providing the first comprehensive empirical evaluation of adaptive feedback gains in near-term quantum error correction.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Integrate experimentally derived dynamic protein interaction data into a transformer-based deep learning model to predict conformational ensembles with greater accuracy than AlphaFold2’s static structure predictions.

BACKGROUND: While AlphaFold2 and similar models achieve remarkable accuracy in predicting single static protein structures, they do not capture the full range of biologically relevant conformational dynamics. Accurately modeling protein conformational ensembles remains an open challenge critical for understanding function, allostery, and ligand binding, yet existing multi-state prediction methods lack explicit integration of dynamic interaction data derived from experiments and simulations.

APPROACH: We propose extending AlphaFold2’s architecture by incorporating residue-residue contact frequency matrices, time-resolved solvent accessibility profiles, and NMR relaxation-derived dynamic features as additional inputs into a transformer encoder. The model predicts a probabilistic conformational ensemble distribution trained with a multi-task loss combining static structure reconstruction and ensemble distribution matching via Earth Mover’s Distance, enabling explicit modeling of conformational heterogeneity.

EXPERIMENT: Benchmark the dynamic model against AlphaFold2 (latest version), RoseTTAFold, and multi-state predictors such as ESMFold and OmegaFold using experimentally validated ensemble datasets from the Protein Ensemble Database (PED) and Dynameomics MD simulations. Evaluate ensemble prediction accuracy using Ensemble RMSD, Ensemble Coverage Score, and Earth Mover’s Distance on a rigorously pre-registered blind test set of non-homologous proteins, applying paired statistical tests (p < 0.01) to determine significant improvements over static predictions.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior multi-state prediction methods, this work explicitly fuses experimentally and simulation-derived dynamic interaction data into deep learning ensemble predictions, supported by rigorous ablation studies and blind benchmarking. It addresses challenges of data noise and heterogeneity with principled integration strategies, offering a theoretically grounded, generalizable approach to capturing protein conformational landscapes beyond static models.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Incorporating experimentally calibrated enhanced sampling molecular simulations into AlphaFold2-generated models improves the conformational ensemble accuracy of intrinsically disordered protein (IDP) regions with transient secondary structure, as validated by integrative NMR and SAXS-based ensemble metrics.

BACKGROUND: AlphaFold2 excels at predicting static folded protein structures but is limited in representing the dynamic conformational heterogeneity of intrinsically disordered proteins and regions (IDRs), which frequently exhibit transient secondary structures. Existing NMR-derived ensembles capture this heterogeneity but often lack integrative approaches that combine high-quality initial models with robust sampling guided by experimental restraints to fully represent IDP conformational landscapes.

APPROACH: Starting from AlphaFold2-generated initial conformations for well-characterized IDPs—alpha-synuclein (UniProt P37840) and the p53 N-terminal domain (UniProt P04637)—we will perform enhanced sampling molecular dynamics simulations (e.g., replica exchange or metadynamics) incorporating harmonic bias potentials derived from experimental NMR observables (chemical shifts, residual dipolar couplings, paramagnetic relaxation enhancements). These restraints will be implemented using PLUMED in GROMACS/AMBER with CHARMM36m force fields, enabling calibrated sampling of conformational ensembles beyond static predictions. Alternative initial ensembles and restraint-only controls will benchmark contributions of sampling and starting structures.

EXPERIMENT: We will quantitatively evaluate ensemble accuracy by comparing back-calculated NMR chemical shifts (via SHIFTX2) and RDC Q-factors (via PALES) against experimental datasets from the Biological Magnetic Resonance Data Bank, targeting >15% RMSD reduction in chemical shifts and >10% Q-factor improvement over both AlphaFold static models and unrestrained MD ensembles. Complementary validation will use radius of gyration distributions and secondary structure propensities matched to SAXS and circular dichroism data, alongside integrative ensemble scoring methods such as ENSEMBLE and Bayesian model comparison. Failure to surpass these benchmarks will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates AlphaFold2 predictions as starting points with experimentally restrained enhanced sampling MD to generate physically meaningful, heterogeneous IDP ensembles—addressing limitations of both deterministic AI predictions and conventional unrestrained simulations—while systematically benchmarking restraint implementation and sampling convergence, thus advancing beyond prior methods that either rely solely on AI or experimental ensemble modeling.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Incorporating evolutionary co-variation data from underutilized large-scale metagenomic sequence datasets (MGnify 2023.10 and UMD 2023) to enrich multiple sequence alignments will significantly improve the accuracy of membrane protein complex structure prediction beyond AlphaFold2 and AlphaFold-Multimer.

BACKGROUND: Accurate prediction of membrane protein complexes remains challenging due to limited structural templates and sparse evolutionary information in standard databases used by AlphaFold pipelines. Current state-of-the-art methods rely on MSAs primarily built from UniRef90 and MGnify, but many deep metagenomic sequences with rich co-evolutionary signals remain underexploited. Leveraging this untapped evolutionary diversity could enhance interface contact prediction and complex assembly modeling, addressing a critical bottleneck in membrane protein structural biology.

APPROACH: We will generate expanded MSAs for membrane protein complexes from the Membrane Protein Complex Benchmark (MPCB) curated from the OPM database (resolution <3.5 Å, ≥2 chains) by integrating homologous sequences extracted with sensitive search tools (HHblits, MMseqs2) against MGnify 2023.10 and UMD 2023 datasets. These enriched MSAs will replace or supplement AlphaFold2 and AlphaFold-Multimer’s default MSAs in their prediction pipelines without initial model retraining. Controls include MSAs matched in sequence depth but derived from non-metagenomic or randomized sequence sets to isolate the impact of metagenomic novelty. Statistical significance will be assessed via paired t-tests and bootstrapping on DockQ and TM-score metrics.

EXPERIMENT: The enhanced MSA pipeline will be benchmarked on the MPCB test set, comparing predicted structures against experimentally determined models using average DockQ score and TM-score as primary metrics, with success defined as ≥10% statistically significant improvement over both AlphaFold2 and AlphaFold-Multimer baselines. Secondary analyses include interface RMSD and inter-subunit contact precision/recall; power analysis will ensure sufficient sample size and statistical rigor.

NOVELTY: This study systematically and explicitly integrates deep metagenomic co-variation information from recently updated, large-scale metagenomic datasets currently underutilized by AlphaFold, coupled with rigorous computational feasibility planning and novel controls to disentangle evolutionary novelty effects. Unlike prior work, it evaluates whether AlphaFold architectures can leverage richer evolutionary inputs without retraining and lays the groundwork for future model adaptation or MSA pipeline redesign targeting membrane protein complex prediction.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Incorporating protein dynamics through enhanced, time-resolved molecular dynamics simulations seeded from AlphaFold2 static predictions will significantly improve the accuracy of predicting functionally relevant conformational ensembles, as validated by comparison to high-quality NMR relaxation data.

BACKGROUND: AlphaFold2 excels at predicting static protein structures but does not capture the intrinsic conformational heterogeneity and dynamics critical for protein function. Existing efforts to refine AlphaFold models via molecular dynamics (MD) have been limited by insufficient sampling and lack of rigorous validation against experimental dynamic observables such as NMR relaxation data. Accurately modeling functionally relevant ensembles remains an open challenge for integrating structure prediction with protein dynamics.

APPROACH: We will generate dynamic conformational ensembles by initiating multiple microsecond-scale explicit solvent MD simulations—augmented with enhanced sampling techniques like accelerated MD or metadynamics—from AlphaFold2-predicted structures. These ensembles will be clustered to identify dominant conformations and weighted to best reproduce experimental conformational distributions. Backbone dynamics will be quantified via simulated order parameters (S²) and benchmarked against NMR relaxation data using correlation analysis, employing rigorous metrics such as ensemble RMSD and the ensemble similarity score (ESS).

EXPERIMENT: Using the NESG NMR ensemble dataset (~50 proteins with experimentally validated structural ensembles and NMR relaxation rates), we will compare dynamic MD-refined AlphaFold ensembles against (1) static AlphaFold predictions, (2) MD ensembles starting from NMR structures, and (3) coarse-grained elastic network models (ANM/ENM). Success requires a statistically significant (p < 0.01, paired Wilcoxon test) and >10% relative improvement in ESS and Pearson correlation between simulated and experimental S² values over the static AlphaFold baseline.

NOVELTY: This study uniquely integrates enhanced sampling MD with AlphaFold predictions and rigorously benchmarks against quantitative NMR relaxation observables, employing advanced ensemble comparison metrics and weighting schemes. It critically assesses inherent biases in starting from AlphaFold static models and establishes robust protocols to validate dynamic predictions beyond prior MD refinement attempts.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating evolutionary covariation signals derived from expanded metagenomic sequence data beyond standard MSAs will improve AlphaFold2’s structure prediction accuracy on low-homology proteins by at least 10% in TM-score.

BACKGROUND:  
AlphaFold2’s predictive accuracy strongly depends on the depth and quality of multiple sequence alignments (MSAs), which are often sparse for proteins with few homologs, limiting performance. Current MSAs primarily use standard sequence databases like UniRef90 and MGnify, which may miss distant evolutionary signals present in recently expanded metagenomic datasets. Leveraging these additional sequences could enhance covariation inference and thus structural modeling, addressing the critical challenge of predicting structures for low-homology proteins.

APPROACH:  
We propose augmenting AlphaFold2’s input MSAs with enriched covariation features extracted from expanded metagenomic sequence databases (MGnify 2023 and MetaClust2) using iterative homology searches (HHblits, JackHMMER) with optimized thresholds. Evolutionary couplings inferred by established methods (GREMLIN, CCMpred) will be integrated as additional pairwise features without modifying AlphaFold2’s architecture. Rigorous sequence filtering and noise-reduction strategies will be applied to mitigate spurious signals, and randomized sequence controls will isolate true evolutionary signal effects.

EXPERIMENT:  
On a curated test set of 150 proteins from the PDB with fewer than 30 effective homologs in standard databases and no overlap with AlphaFold training or CASP sets, we will compare three conditions: (a) AlphaFold2 with standard MSAs, (b) AlphaFold2 with MSAs augmented by expanded metagenomic sequences, and (c) AlphaFold2 with metagenomic-only MSAs. The primary metric is the mean TM-score of predicted versus experimental structures, analyzed with confidence intervals and statistical tests to assess continuous improvements. A ≥10% relative TM-score increase over baseline (a) will support the hypothesis, while lower or nonsignificant gains will falsify it.

NOVELTY:  
This work systematically evaluates the impact of recently expanded metagenomic datasets on AlphaFold2 accuracy specifically for low-homology proteins, with explicit incorporation of evolutionary covariation signals as additional features rather than architecture changes. It uniquely combines rigorous statistical controls, noise management, and ablation studies to disentangle true signal benefits from noise, going beyond prior MSA augmentation studies and addressing practical computational constraints and quality filtering in large-scale metagenomic integration.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Integrating temporal embeddings constructed from static observational data prior to causal discovery enhances latent confounder identification compared to standard static methods, by implicitly encoding hidden temporal dependencies absent in snapshots.

BACKGROUND: Identifying latent confounders from purely static observational data remains a fundamental challenge in causal discovery, especially when time information is unrecorded or unavailable. Existing methods such as FCI and KiNCD operate on static features and often miss confounding signals that manifest through latent temporal dynamics. There is a need for approaches that leverage temporal structure assumptions to improve confounder detection without requiring explicitly recorded time series data.

APPROACH: We propose generating temporal embeddings from static observational data via a sliding window approach that creates lagged versions (lags 1 to 5) of each variable, thus implicitly reconstructing hidden temporal dependencies under stationarity assumptions. This temporally enriched dataset is then analyzed with a modified Fast Causal Inference (FCI) algorithm tailored to detect latent confounders. To rule out improvements from mere dimensionality increase or noise, we introduce rigorous controls including randomized/shuffled lag features and dimensionality-matched non-temporal augmentations.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate our approach on synthetic benchmarks from the CauseMe Challenge 2023 (scenarios s3 and s4) featuring known nonlinear causal graphs with explicit latent confounders, the Alarm Bayesian network with artificially introduced lagged features to simulate temporal embedding, and the real-world PhysioNet Sepsis dataset where unobserved patient states induce latent confounding. Performance is measured by Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) focused on latent confounder edges, false positive rates, and overall graph accuracy, averaged over multiple seeds. The hypothesis is falsified if temporal embedding fails to improve latent confounder SHD by at least 10% over standard FCI and KiNCD baselines or if it performs worse.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing time series causal discovery methods that require genuine temporal data, our work innovatively applies temporal embedding to static data under explicit stationarity assumptions, combined with rigorous controls to isolate true temporal effects. This formalizes and validates a novel preprocessing step that enhances latent confounder identifiability beyond state-of-the-art static methods like KiNCD, expanding the toolkit for causal discovery in domains lacking explicit time annotations.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating domain-specific causal constraints as hard masks and asymmetric penalties into nonlinear causal discovery algorithms like KiNCD significantly reduces false positive edges on high-dimensional observational biomedical data without increasing false negatives or overall structural errors.

BACKGROUND: Nonlinear causal discovery from purely observational data often suffers from high false positive rates, especially in high-dimensional biological systems with complex interactions. While domain knowledge is available for many biomedical networks, existing nonlinear discovery methods rarely integrate such constraints directly into continuous optimization frameworks, limiting reliability and interpretability. Robust methods that leverage expert-derived causal constraints are needed to improve accuracy and trustworthiness in practical scientific applications.

APPROACH: We extend KiNCD by integrating domain-specific causal constraints derived from expert knowledge as hard adjacency masks (forbidden edges) and asymmetric penalty terms (fixed causal directions) within its continuous optimization framework. This constrained KiNCD method allows controlled enforcement of prior knowledge during nonlinear causal structure learning, with additional experiments testing robustness to imperfect, noisy, or partial constraints through ablation studies and soft/hard penalty variants.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on two biomedical observational datasets with established causal ground truth and domain constraints: (1) the Sachs phospho-protein signaling dataset (~11 continuous variables) and (2) a curated ~50-gene subset of the GEO GSE2034 breast cancer gene expression dataset with known pathway constraints. Performance is measured by False Positive Rate (FPR), Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), and Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC), comparing constrained KiNCD against unconstrained KiNCD, NOTEARS-MLP, and ANM, with rigorous statistical testing over multiple replicates. The hypothesis is supported if constrained KiNCD reduces FPR by ≥20% relative to baselines without significant increases (p<0.05) in false negatives or SHD, and falsified if FPR reduction is <10% or errors increase >10%.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates domain constraints directly into a nonlinear, continuous optimization causal discovery framework with formalized hard masking and asymmetric penalties, providing theoretical insights into identifiability and bias-variance trade-offs. Unlike previous methods relying solely on data-driven search or post hoc constraint application, we systematically evaluate robustness to constraint imperfections and demonstrate practical improvements on real high-dimensional biomedical data, advancing both methodological rigor and scientific applicability.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating rigorously validated domain-specific causal invariances as explicit constraints into nonlinear causal discovery algorithms significantly enhances edge direction accuracy on truly high-dimensional observational datasets compared to purely data-driven methods.

BACKGROUND: Inferring accurate causal directions from high-dimensional, nonlinear observational data remains a critical challenge due to inherent identifiability issues and complex dependencies. Existing state-of-the-art methods like KiNCD achieve strong performance but often disregard rich domain knowledge about causal invariances, such as stable causal relations under perturbations or mechanistic constraints, which could improve direction inference. However, the practical utility and theoretical impact of integrating such domain-specific invariances in nonlinear causal discovery remain underexplored.

APPROACH: We propose extending the KiNCD framework by explicitly encoding well-defined, independently validated domain-specific causal invariances—such as conditionally invariant causal links, monotonicity, and conservation laws—as penalty terms and hard constraints in the continuous optimization objective. This approach includes rigorous ablation studies comparing full invariance integration, randomized invariance constraints, and no-invariance baselines to isolate their precise contribution. We also develop formal theoretical analyses demonstrating improved identifiability and estimator consistency under these invariances, along with robustness characterizations to invariance misspecification.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on truly high-dimensional datasets with reliable, unambiguous ground truth causal graphs and domain invariances, including the Sachs et al. (2005) protein signaling network and the Spellman et al. (1998) CHO cell-cycle gene expression dataset, supplemented by a synthetic high-dimensional benchmark specifically designed to encode verified nonlinear causal invariances. Performance is assessed via edge direction accuracy (EDA), Structural Hamming Distance (SHD), precision, recall, and computational efficiency, with success defined as at least a 10% absolute EDA improvement over KiNCD and key baselines (Orthogonal Structure Search and S-FCI) at p < 0.05 significance.

NOVELTY: This work meaningfully advances nonlinear causal discovery by tightly integrating rigorously validated domain-specific causal invariances into a continuous optimization framework, supported by new theoretical guarantees and comprehensive ablation studies, thus going beyond prior work that either omits domain knowledge or uses it heuristically without formal characterization or robust empirical isolation of its benefits.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating multivariate dynamic time warping (DTW) as a global-consistent preprocessing step to dynamically align nonlinear temporal observational data will significantly improve causal discovery accuracy over existing static or fixed-lag alignment methods.

BACKGROUND: Accurate causal discovery from nonlinear temporal observational datasets is hindered by variable and nonlinear time delays that static alignment methods often fail to capture, limiting identifiability and precision. Current state-of-the-art causal discovery algorithms (e.g., TiMINO, DYNOTEARS, KiNCD) assume fixed or known temporal lags, which do not accommodate complex temporal warping present in many real-world time series, thus motivating the need for dynamic alignment techniques that preserve causal semantics.

APPROACH: We propose to apply pairwise multivariate DTW with novel computational scalability heuristics—such as candidate edge pruning and joint alignment constraints enforcing global temporal consistency—to preprocess time series by optimally warping and resampling target variables. This preprocessing transforms time series to a temporally aligned domain where causal dependencies are more stationary and identifiable, enabling existing nonlinear causal discovery methods (TiMINO, DYNOTEARS, KiNCD) to achieve improved accuracy. The approach includes theoretical analysis demonstrating how DTW preserves causal identifiability relative to time-delay embedding and lag-adaptive methods, and control experiments using randomized warping to validate the causal relevance of DTW alignment.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on two nonlinear temporal causal discovery benchmarks with ground-truth graphs: (1) the Simulated Nonlinear Temporal Causal Dataset (Peters et al., 2017) with variable time-lagged nonlinear interactions, and (2) the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset (2012) featuring complex sensor temporal misalignments. Performance is measured by reduction in Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) and increase in Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) for edge detection, averaged over 10 randomized runs. The hypothesis is supported if DTW preprocessing achieves ≥15% SHD reduction and ≥10% AUPRC improvement over the best baseline without DTW, with statistical significance (p < 0.05); failure to do so or performance degradation beyond 5% falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior causal discovery methods that assume static or fixed temporal lags, our method incorporates dynamic temporal alignment via multivariate DTW with explicit global consistency enforcement and theoretical guarantees on preserving causal identifiability. This approach uniquely integrates scalable DTW preprocessing with nonlinear causal discovery frameworks, accompanied by rigorous experimental controls and validation to isolate the distinct benefits of dynamic alignment beyond existing lag-adaptive or embedding techniques.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate domain-specific graphical priors as soft constraints into continuous optimization-based causal discovery methods to improve accuracy in identifying causal directions from high-dimensional observational data.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurately discovering causal structures from purely observational, high-dimensional data remains a significant challenge, especially when nonlinear relationships and latent confounders exist. Existing state-of-the-art nonlinear optimization approaches like KiNCD and NOTEARS generally do not explicitly leverage domain knowledge, potentially limiting their performance and interpretability in scientific applications where reliable prior information is available.

APPROACH:  
We extend continuous optimization causal discovery frameworks (e.g., NOTEARS with nonlinear extensions) by incorporating domain-specific graphical priors—such as known or forbidden edges derived from expert knowledge—as soft penalty terms in the objective function. This formulation balances data fidelity and adherence to priors via a tunable hyperparameter, \(\lambda\), optimized through rigorous hyperparameter search and sensitivity analysis to ensure robustness against prior misspecification.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the method on three benchmark datasets with ground-truth causal graphs and validated priors: the MAGIC Gamma Telescope dataset, the SynTReN synthetic gene regulatory network data, and the Sachs et al. (2005) flow cytometry data. Performance will be measured using Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) and True Positive Rate (TPR) against baselines including KiNCD, nonlinear NOTEARS, and S-FCI, with statistical significance tested over 20+ random seeds. Ablation studies with no and noisy priors will assess the true impact and robustness of domain knowledge. Success is defined as at least a 15% SHD reduction and 10% TPR increase relative to the best baseline on at least two datasets.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which either ignores domain priors or uses them as hard constraints, our approach introduces a principled, theoretically motivated soft penalty framework with explicit hyperparameter analysis and validation to integrate domain knowledge into nonlinear causal discovery. This method advances beyond KiNCD and existing continuous optimization methods by systematically quantifying the value of domain priors and providing guidelines to avoid overconstraining, thereby enhancing both accuracy and interpretability in real-world high-dimensional settings.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrating bio-inspired multi-compartment dendritic computation units within neuromorphic synapses reduces system-level energy consumption by at least 25% without degrading inference accuracy on complex spatiotemporal pattern recognition tasks.

BACKGROUND: Existing neuromorphic architectures primarily rely on point-neuron synaptic models that underutilize dendritic nonlinearities observed in biological neurons, limiting potential energy efficiency gains. Addressing how dendritic subunit computations can be harnessed at scale on neuromorphic hardware remains an open problem, particularly in balancing additional computational overhead with overall system-level energy savings.

APPROACH: We augment a spiking convolutional neuromorphic network baseline (modeled after the 2016 “Convolutional networks for fast, energy-efficient neuromorphic computing” architecture) by embedding event-driven, multi-compartment dendritic units that perform local nonlinear integrations (e.g., multiplicative and thresholding operations) compatible with low-power neuromorphic platforms such as Intel Loihi. Both baseline and dendritic-augmented models undergo symmetrical, blinded hyperparameter optimization and include ablation studies to isolate the energy and accuracy contributions of dendritic nonlinearities.

EXPERIMENT: Using the N-MNIST and DVS128 Gesture datasets, we will measure system-level energy per inference via platform-level instrumentation with rigorous baseline controls and repeated trials, alongside classification accuracy on test splits; the hypothesis is confirmed if the dendritic model reduces energy consumption by ≥25% relative to baseline while maintaining accuracy within 1% absolute on both datasets.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously demonstrates hardware-feasible dendritic computation integration with detailed energy measurement protocols and unbiased hyperparameter tuning, advancing beyond prior neuromorphic synapse and multi-compartment neuron models by explicitly quantifying system-level energy savings without accuracy trade-offs on complex event-based tasks.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrating memristive devices with simplified bio-inspired dendritic processing circuits can reduce neuromorphic system energy consumption by at least 20% compared to baseline single-layer synaptic arrays on sparse event-driven datasets, without significant accuracy loss, validated through combined simulation and small-scale experimental prototyping.

BACKGROUND: Current neuromorphic hardware predominantly relies on single-layer memristive synaptic arrays that directly map spike inputs to outputs, limiting energy efficiency especially on sparse, low-complexity datasets common in event-driven vision tasks. While dendritic processing in biological neurons enables local nonlinear integration to reduce redundant synaptic activity and energy use, its practical incorporation into memristive neuromorphic systems remains underexplored, particularly considering device variability and integration overheads.

APPROACH: We propose to design and simulate memristor-based dendritic subcircuits implementing lightweight nonlinear functions (e.g., thresholding and quadratic transformations) that locally preprocess synaptic inputs before aggregation, thereby reducing total switching activity and energy. A rigorous energy accounting framework including peripheral circuitry and control overhead will be developed, alongside calibration protocols to mitigate device variability. Importantly, a small-scale physical prototype of the dendritic circuits will be fabricated and characterized to validate simulation assumptions and assess real-world variability and parasitic effects.

EXPERIMENT: The approach will be evaluated on the N-MNIST and DVS Gesture datasets using two hardware-modeled architectures: a baseline single-layer memristive synaptic array and the proposed dendritic-enhanced network, both trained with spike-timing dependent plasticity. Energy per inference—including synaptic operations and dendritic overhead—will be measured through device-level circuit simulations corroborated by prototype characterization. Success requires at least a 20% energy reduction with less than 1% accuracy degradation relative to baseline; failure is defined as less than 10% energy savings or more than 5% accuracy loss despite mitigation efforts.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior works focusing solely on synaptic device innovations or algorithmic sparsity, this study uniquely combines memristive hardware dendritic nonlinearities with rigorous overhead quantification and experimental validation, establishing a comprehensive theoretical and practical framework that demonstrates how bio-inspired dendritic processing can concretely reduce energy consumption on sparse event-driven datasets without sacrificing performance.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrating bio-inspired multi-compartment dendritic processing units with energy-efficient neuromorphic synapses will reduce overall inference energy consumption by at least 20% during complex spatiotemporal pattern recognition tasks without sacrificing classification accuracy beyond 1%.

BACKGROUND: Neuromorphic computing seeks to emulate brain-like efficiency in processing spatiotemporal sensory data, but current architectures often overlook the computational advantages of dendritic nonlinearities, which biologically enable local integration and sparse activation. Existing synapse-focused energy optimizations achieve gains but may plateau without incorporating dendritic computation, leaving an open problem of how to leverage neuron compartmentalization to further reduce power while maintaining accuracy on real-world neuromorphic workloads.

APPROACH: We propose augmenting state-of-the-art convolutional spiking neural networks with bio-inspired multi-compartment dendritic processing units implemented as nonlinear, compartmentalized leaky integrate-and-fire neurons with localized plasticity, positioned upstream of energy-efficient synaptic arrays such as ferroelectric synaptic transistors. This architecture mimics dendritic branches’ local integration to sparsify downstream synaptic activations and reduce redundant spiking, thereby lowering energy consumption during inference.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate three model variants on the Neuromorphic MNIST (N-MNIST) and DVS128 Gesture datasets: (1) baseline convolutional SNN on Loihi or SpiNNaker hardware simulator without dendrites, (2) baseline augmented with energy-efficient ferroelectric DropConnect synapses, and (3) the dendritic model with multi-compartment nonlinear units integrated before synapses. Energy consumption per inference will be measured in picojoules using cycle-accurate power models cross-validated against silicon data, targeting ≥20% reduction relative to the best baseline, while classification accuracy must remain within 1% of baseline performance. Incremental controls with compartmentalized neurons lacking nonlinear dendritic dynamics will isolate dendritic contributions. Failure to meet energy or accuracy criteria across multiple cross-validated runs will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work focusing solely on synaptic device innovations or flat spiking neuron models, this study explicitly integrates biologically inspired dendritic processing units with state-of-the-art energy-efficient synapses, supported by a mechanistic theoretical framework predicting energy gains from localized nonlinear integration. This comprehensive approach addresses practical hardware realizability and scalability, providing the first rigorous evaluation of dendritic computation’s direct impact on neuromorphic energy efficiency and accuracy in complex temporal-spatial pattern recognition.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Memristive synapses with carefully characterized and constrained stochastic switching behavior can improve learning robustness and reduce training energy in spiking neural networks under controlled variability, compared to deterministic synapses.

BACKGROUND:  
Neuromorphic computing faces challenges in balancing energy efficiency, learning robustness, and device variability, particularly when using memristive synapses whose inherent stochastic switching impacts plasticity. While deterministic synapse models simplify control, they often lead to higher energy consumption or reduced adaptability. Exploiting constrained stochastic switching could harness device variability beneficially, but its precise impact on learning efficacy and energy remains underexplored with realistic device and system-level validation.

APPROACH:  
We propose modeling memristive synapses in SNNs with stochastic switching governed by experimentally calibrated probability distributions modulated via adaptive pulse amplitude control to constrain variability within target thresholds. This approach enables controlled stochasticity during synaptic updates, balancing accuracy and energy use by leveraging device physics-based switching energy models and pulse modulation feedback. Validation is augmented with partial hardware experiments on physical memristive devices implementing these control schemes to ground simulation assumptions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train SNNs on the N-MNIST and Fashion-MNIST datasets comparing three synapse models—(1) deterministic ideal switching, (2) unconstrained high-variance stochastic switching, and (3) constrained stochastic switching with adaptive pulse modulation—using spike-timing-dependent plasticity and backpropagation-through-time algorithms. Metrics include classification accuracy (target within 1% of deterministic baseline), total training energy estimated from device-level switching physics (expecting ≥10% reduction), convergence speed, and robustness measured by variance over multiple runs. Statistical significance will be assessed via ANOVA.

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously integrates experimentally validated stochastic switching distributions with adaptive hardware-level control to constrain variability, bridging device physics and network learning dynamics beyond prior stochasticity exploitation frameworks. It uniquely combines simulation and real-device validation to quantitatively link constrained stochasticity with improved energy-accuracy tradeoffs in neuromorphic systems, differentiating it from existing deterministic or unconstrained stochastic approaches.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Incorporating biologically inspired astrocyte-like modulatory units into spiking neural network architectures will reduce overall energy consumption by at least 20% without degrading classification accuracy by more than 1% across neuromorphic benchmarks.

BACKGROUND: Current neuromorphic computing approaches primarily focus on neuron-centric or device-level improvements to reduce energy consumption but often overlook the potential of neuro-glial interactions found in biological brains. Astrocytes modulate synaptic activity on slower timescales and may enable adaptive, spike-efficient computations, presenting an unexplored avenue to lower metabolic costs in neuromorphic systems without sacrificing accuracy.

APPROACH: We propose to augment a baseline convolutional spiking neural network (Spiking ResNet) with biologically inspired astrocyte-like units that dynamically modulate synaptic plasticity based on local spike activity, implementing slower-timescale gliotransmission-inspired adaptive thresholding and temporal smoothing. This astrocyte modulation will be rigorously modeled and differentiated from standard neuromodulatory frameworks to explicitly target redundant spiking reduction and energy savings.

EXPERIMENT: The astrocyte-augmented Spiking ResNet will be evaluated on the event-driven N-MNIST and DVS128 Gesture datasets, benchmarking inference energy consumption (in picojoules) via cycle-accurate hardware simulations and neuromorphic simulators with energy modeling, against the original Spiking ResNet baseline and the hardware-implemented DropConnect synaptic transistor model (2025). Energy savings ≥20% with ≤1% accuracy loss will confirm the hypothesis; savings <10% or accuracy losses >1% will falsify it.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely integrates astrocyte-inspired modulatory mechanisms into neuromorphic architectures with realistic energy modeling and hardware validation, providing a mechanistic link between neuro-glial dynamics and spike-efficient computation—advancing beyond existing neuron-centric, optoelectronic synapse, or stochastic hardware DropConnect approaches.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Integrating experimentally validated protein conformational ensembles from the Protein Ensemble Database into generative AI molecular design pipelines will improve in vitro target specificity for dynamic binding-site proteins compared to static-structure-based models, as demonstrated by statistically significant increases in binding enrichment factors from targeted specificity assays.

BACKGROUND: Conventional AI-driven molecular design typically relies on static protein structures, neglecting the intrinsic conformational dynamics that critically influence ligand binding specificity. This limitation hampers the generation of molecules with high target selectivity, especially for proteins with flexible binding pockets such as HIV-1 protease and p38 MAP kinase. Addressing this gap by incorporating experimentally derived protein ensembles could enhance the relevance and precision of generative drug design.

APPROACH: We will develop a conditional generative AI pipeline that encodes multiple experimentally validated protein conformations from PED using graph neural network or 3D CNN embeddings of dynamic binding pockets. Molecules generated by fine-tuned graph-based VAEs or Transformer models will be iteratively scored via ensemble docking across all conformers using AutoDock Vina, with aggregated docking energies guiding reinforcement learning updates. This ensemble-based scoring contrasts with a baseline pipeline leveraging only a single high-resolution static structure.

EXPERIMENT: Using PED ensembles for HIV-1 protease (PED00054) and p38 MAP kinase (PED00091), we will generate sets of candidate molecules from both models, prioritize compounds based on aggregated docking scores and predicted specificity metrics, and synthesize a down-selected subset. Target specificity will be quantitatively assessed via medium-throughput in vitro binding assays (e.g., SPR, fluorescence polarization) against on-target proteins and structurally related off-targets—also characterized by conformational ensembles—to measure enrichment factors and IC50 specificity ratios. Statistical significance (p < 0.05) of at least 20% improvement in specificity by the ensemble model will validate the hypothesis; failure to exceed baseline performance or meaningful differences falsifies it.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior generative AI drug discovery studies relying solely on static structures or single-target docking, this work explicitly integrates experimentally derived multi-conformational ensembles into both molecular generation and scoring, with rigorous prospective experimental validation against orthogonal off-target controls. It addresses ensemble representativeness limitations by grounding conformational diversity in validated experimental data and incorporates complementary scoring validations to robustly link docking metrics with measured binding specificity.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Integrating indigenous Nigerian medicinal plant biochemical profiles as explicit multi-domain embeddings into Transformer-based generative AI models will yield drug candidates with a significantly higher in vitro bioactivity hit rate (≥15% improvement, p < 0.05) against neglected tropical diseases than candidates generated from global molecular datasets alone.

BACKGROUND: Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) such as leishmaniasis, Chagas disease, and schistosomiasis remain under-addressed due to limited drug discovery efforts and chemical space exploration. While generative AI models have accelerated drug candidate design from global molecular databases, the potential of indigenous medicinal plant biochemical knowledge to enrich chemical diversity and bioactivity profiles relevant to NTDs remains underexplored and poorly quantified. This gap limits the discovery of novel, efficacious compounds tailored to NTDs prevalent in regions like Nigeria.

APPROACH: We will develop a multi-domain Transformer variational autoencoder (VAE) that jointly encodes molecular structure, indigenous biochemical metadata (e.g., plant source, phytochemical class, traditional use), and bioactivity annotations to generate novel small molecules. By integrating curated indigenous datasets from ANPDB and AfroDb with global datasets (ZINC15 and ChEMBL compounds active against NTD targets), our model leverages complementary chemical space and domain-specific features. Bayesian optimization over the learned latent space, constrained by in silico ADMET and synthetic accessibility filters, will prioritize candidates for synthesis and in vitro testing.

EXPERIMENT: Four generative models will be trained and compared: (1) Indigenous-Integrated Model (IIM) trained on combined indigenous and global data with multi-domain embeddings; (2) Indigenous-Only Model (IOM) trained solely on indigenous data; (3) Global-Only Model (GOM) trained solely on global data; and (4) MolGAN baseline trained on global data. The top 500 molecules per model after property-driven optimization will be synthesized or procured and assessed in standardized, replicated bioassays against Leishmania donovani, Trypanosoma cruzi, and Schistosoma mansoni. Success is a statistically significant (p < 0.05) ≥15% increase in hit rate (IC50 or EC50 ≤ 10 μM) for IIM over all other models, falsified if not met or if bioactivity distributions overlap substantially.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely combines indigenous biochemical metadata as structured, multi-domain embeddings in generative AI, supported by rigorous ablation controls and standardized cross-disease bioactivity assays, thereby empirically quantifying the added value of indigenous chemical space for NTD drug discovery—advancing beyond prior ethnopharmacology-informed models by explicitly demonstrating non-redundant chemical diversity and bioactivity gains.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating high-quality, rigorously curated indigenous biochemical pathway data from Nigerian medicinal plants with extensive global molecular datasets in a multi-modal Transformer-based generative AI framework enables the design of novel drug candidates with significantly improved in vitro efficacy against region-specific *Plasmodium falciparum* and *Mycobacterium tuberculosis* variants.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI drug discovery models primarily rely on large global chemical and bioactivity datasets, which often overlook region-specific biochemical contexts and pathogen variant vulnerabilities. Indigenous biochemical pathway data derived from Nigerian ethnopharmacology encapsulates unique metabolic and signaling interactions not represented in global databases, offering complementary molecular features potentially critical for targeting local pathogen variants. Incorporating such ethnobiological knowledge promises to address dataset biases and improve the regional specificity and efficacy of AI-generated therapeutics.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-input generative Transformer architecture that fuses global chemical embeddings from ZINC-15 and ChEMBL with graph neural network (GNN)-encoded embeddings of indigenous biochemical pathways representing metabolite-enzyme interactions from Nigerian medicinal plants. Conditioned on pathogen variant-specific protein targets, such as *P. falciparum* DHFR and *M. tuberculosis* protein variants, the model will employ reinforcement learning with docking score proxies as rewards to bias molecule generation toward scaffolds enriched in indigenous biochemical motifs. This multi-modal embedding fusion is theoretically justified by demonstrated non-redundancy and biochemical uniqueness of indigenous data, validated through preliminary correlation analyses linking indigenous metabolites to pathogen target vulnerabilities.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will generate and synthesize the top 100 unique molecules per model variant (integrated pathway+global, global-only, indigenous-only, and negative controls with scrambled pathway data), testing them in triplicate in standardized in vitro bioassays measuring IC50 against Nigerian pathogen variants. Statistical significance (p < 0.05, paired t-test) and a minimum 20% IC50 improvement over the global-only baseline will validate the hypothesis. Additional evaluation includes docking scores, predicted ADMET properties, and cross-validation on pathogen variants from other regions to isolate the effect of indigenous data integration.

NOVELTY:  
This study uniquely combines rigorously validated indigenous biochemical pathway data with global datasets in a multi-modal generative AI framework, explicitly targeting region-specific pathogen variants with stratified experimental controls. Unlike prior works that broadly incorporate local data or focus on global targets, this approach substantiates the causal value of ethnopharmacological knowledge in AI-driven drug design through statistically robust in vitro validation and controls for dataset biases, advancing precision drug discovery for underrepresented populations.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Generative AI models trained on integrated synthetic chemistry reaction data and indigenous Nigerian medicinal plant compounds will generate novel drug candidates with significantly higher in vitro bioactivity against neglected tropical diseases than models trained solely on global molecular databases.

BACKGROUND:  
Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) such as leishmaniasis and trypanosomiasis disproportionately impact underserved populations but suffer from a paucity of effective, affordable treatments. Existing generative AI drug discovery approaches typically rely on global chemical databases that lack regional ethnopharmacological specificity and contextual synthetic information, limiting their ability to produce novel, bioactive candidates tailored to NTDs. Incorporating indigenous medicinal knowledge alongside synthetic reaction data may enrich chemical diversity and improve drug candidate relevance and synthesizability.

APPROACH:  
We will curate and harmonize two heterogeneous datasets—a USPTO patent-derived synthetic reaction corpus (1976–2023) and a comprehensive indigenous Nigerian medicinal plant compound dataset from AfroDb plus ethnopharmacological literature—and integrate them for training a Transformer-based generative model (Chemformer architecture) generating SMILES molecules. A parallel model will be trained on global-only molecular datasets (ChEMBL 31 + ZINC15 drug-like subset) under matched training regimes. Both models will undergo reinforcement learning with a composite reward function combining drug-likeness (QED), synthetic accessibility (SAS), and target-specific bioactivity proxies derived from predictive models trained on known NTD actives, to guide molecule generation towards biologically relevant chemical space.

EXPERIMENT:  
Each model will generate 10,000 novel candidates, from which the top 500 molecules per model will be selected based on reward scores and synthetic feasibility validated by expert retrosynthetic analysis and commercial availability checks. A blinded subset of 50 compounds per model will be synthesized or procured and tested in standardized, high-throughput, biosafety-compliant in vitro assays against Trypanosoma brucei and Leishmania donovani, including rigorously validated positive and negative controls, to quantify hit rates (IC50 ≤ 1 μM) and potency distributions. Statistical comparison via Fisher’s exact test will determine whether the combined-data-trained model significantly outperforms the global-only baseline, falsifying the hypothesis if no improvement is observed.

NOVELTY:  
This study uniquely integrates synthetic reaction knowledge with indigenous medicinal chemistry data to enhance generative AI-driven drug discovery for NTDs, moving beyond generic global chemical space and purely drug-likeness-guided approaches. The combination of ethnopharmacological data, synthetic feasibility validation, target-specific reward augmentation, and rigorous bioactivity benchmarking in infectious disease-relevant assays establishes a comprehensive and translationally focused framework not previously demonstrated.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Integrating GPU-accelerated Density Functional Theory (DFT)-derived quantum chemical descriptors into a Transformer-based variational autoencoder for molecular generation will yield drug candidates with significantly improved in vitro binding affinity against EGFR compared to models trained solely on classical cheminformatics descriptors.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery typically rely on classical molecular descriptors that may insufficiently capture electronic and quantum mechanical properties critical for accurate binding affinity prediction. While quantum chemical features such as HOMO-LUMO gaps and partial charges are theoretically more informative of molecular interactions, their integration into generative pipelines remains underexplored due to computational cost and uncertain predictive benefit. Demonstrating that quantum-derived descriptors enhance generative model outputs could advance rational molecular design beyond traditional heuristic features.

APPROACH: We will develop two Transformer-based variational autoencoder generative pipelines trained on the ChEMBL 29 dataset filtered for EGFR inhibitors (>10,000 molecules with measured IC50/Ki). The “Quantum-AI” model incorporates DFT-calculated quantum descriptors (e.g., HOMO-LUMO gap, dipole moment, atomic partial charges) computed at the B3LYP/6-31G* level with rigorous conformer sampling on a GPU-accelerated cloud platform, integrated as additional latent conditioning variables. The “Classical-AI” baseline uses only classical descriptors including ECFP4 fingerprints, LogP, TPSA, and molecular weight. Both models share identical architectures and training protocols to isolate the effect of quantum features.
