EXPERIMENT: We will generate and synthesize the top 100 candidate molecules from each pipeline ranked by model-predicted binding affinity and synthetic accessibility scores. Binding affinities will be measured via standardized in vitro assays (surface plasmon resonance and fluorescence polarization) against recombinant human EGFR. Statistical comparison of median IC50 values will be performed with a Mann-Whitney U test, targeting at least a 20% improvement (e.g., median IC50 reduction from 100 nM to ≤80 nM) with p < 0.05. Diversity, synthetic feasibility, and novelty metrics will be controlled and reported to exclude bias and confirm genuine performance gains attributable to quantum descriptors.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which primarily leverages classical molecular fingerprints or sequence-based generative models without quantum inputs, this study systematically incorporates robust, conformer-validated DFT quantum features into a state-of-the-art Transformer VAE architecture for drug design. It uniquely couples scalable quantum chemistry with deep generative modeling and experimentally validates the impact of quantum descriptors on binding affinity improvement, thereby advancing the methodological and practical frontiers of AI-driven molecular discovery.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
In heterogeneous federated learning with non-IID data, client-side adaptive model pruning guided by local data distribution combined with a mask-aware aggregation method reduces communication overhead by at least 20% without exceeding a 2% accuracy drop compared to fixed-architecture baselines.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning (FL) on heterogeneous clients with non-IID data suffers from excessive communication costs and accuracy degradation when using fixed global model architectures. Existing methods either maintain uniform model sizes or apply generic adaptation schemes that inadequately address personalized pruning and the challenge of aggregating structurally diverse client models, limiting scalability and efficiency.

APPROACH:  
Each client performs data-dependent adaptive pruning of a global base model using a lightweight sensitivity analysis weighted by local class frequencies to remove redundant neurons or filters before training. The pruned models are communicated along with pruning masks and architecture metadata to the server, which uses a rigorously designed mask-aware Federated Averaging algorithm that efficiently aggregates heterogeneous architectures across 100+ clients. The approach is benchmarked against fixed-size FedAvg, FedProx, FedGKD, and the recent Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation method.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on CIFAR-10 partitioned via a Dirichlet distribution (α=0.5) across 100 clients for image classification and the Shakespeare dataset from LEAF for next-character prediction. Communication overhead is comprehensively measured including weights, pruning masks, metadata, and protocol overhead, with success defined as ≥20% reduction relative to fixed FedAvg. Accuracy is measured after 200 communication rounds on held-out test sets; no more than a 2% absolute accuracy drop compared to fixed-size baselines validates the hypothesis. Statistical significance and ablation studies isolate mask-aware aggregation and pruning effects under IID and non-IID conditions.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines personalized, data-driven adaptive pruning with a theoretically and empirically validated mask-aware aggregation mechanism explicitly designed for heterogeneous architectures at scale. Unlike prior FL pruning or distillation methods, it rigorously quantifies comprehensive communication costs and provides strong theoretical and ablation evidence linking pruning adaptivity to improved efficiency and accuracy in non-IID federated learning.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporate client-specific adaptive model splitting ratios dynamically determined by quantified local data heterogeneity to reduce total communication cost by at least 20% compared to uniform split federated learning under identical accuracy constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with split models suffers from high communication costs, especially under realistic heterogeneous and non-IID client data distributions. Existing Split Federated Learning (SFL) methods typically assume uniform model partitioning across clients, which fails to exploit client-specific data heterogeneity to optimize communication efficiency. Addressing this gap is critical to improving scalability and resource utilization in federated systems with heterogeneous clients.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dynamic adaptive splitting scheme where each client periodically computes a heterogeneity score—using class distribution entropy or Wasserstein distance relative to the global distribution—and adjusts its model split ratio accordingly. Clients with highly heterogeneous data offload more computation to the server by reducing their local model portion, thus transmitting smaller intermediate representations and lowering communication. The split ratios are updated at controlled intervals with smoothing constraints to ensure training stability, balancing communication savings against client computation.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets partitioned via Dirichlet distributions (α=0.1 and α=0.5) to simulate varying non-IID scenarios. Baselines include Uniform Split SFL with fixed 50-50 splits, HeteroSFL with heterogeneous clients but uniform splits, and centralized training as an accuracy upper bound. Metrics include total communication volume (MB), test accuracy on a global held-out set (±1% tolerance), convergence speed, and client computational load measured in FLOPs. Success requires at least 20% communication reduction without accuracy degradation beyond 1%.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as HeteroSFL that acknowledge client heterogeneity but maintain uniform splits, our method actively adapts model partitioning per client based on rigorously quantified data heterogeneity metrics with controlled dynamic updates. We concurrently analyze communication-computation trade-offs, provide ablation studies on update protocols, and theoretically justify heterogeneity-guided splitting, thus establishing a stable, reproducible framework with explicit bounds on accuracy preservation.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporate client-specific data complexity metrics combining label entropy and feature-space clustering difficulty to dynamically adjust local model capacity in federated learning, reducing convergence time on heterogeneous non-IID datasets compared to uniform architectures.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning with heterogeneous clients and non-IID data suffers from slow convergence and degraded performance when using uniform local model architectures, as client data complexity and capacity needs vary widely. Existing adaptive methods like FedGKD and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation address heterogeneity via knowledge distillation but maintain fixed model sizes, limiting their ability to fully exploit client-specific capacity demands. Efficiently tailoring local model complexity to data characteristics remains an open challenge to accelerate convergence while preserving accuracy.

APPROACH: We propose to first compute a robust client-specific data complexity metric by combining local label distribution entropy and silhouette scores derived from embeddings produced by a lightweight, pre-shared feature extractor; this metric then guides dynamic adjustment of local CNN model capacity—e.g., varying numbers of layers or neurons per client. A meta-architecture on the server aggregates heterogeneous client models via partial parameter sharing of a shared representation layer and knowledge distillation losses to align outputs despite architectural differences. This enables efficient training rounds tailored to individual client complexity.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on FEMNIST and CIFAR-10 with Dirichlet (α=0.5) partitions, comparing convergence time measured in communication rounds and wall-clock time needed to reach 85% accuracy on FEMNIST and 70% on CIFAR-10 against FedAvg, FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation baselines. We perform ablation studies isolating the complexity metric components, include static heterogeneous-capacity controls, and analyze overhead of complexity computation and aggregation to ensure net efficiency gains across 10 independent runs per setup with rigorous statistical testing.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that maintains fixed local architectures or focuses solely on knowledge distillation, our method uniquely integrates a theoretically and empirically validated data complexity metric to dynamically tailor local model capacity per client, coupled with a novel heterogeneous model aggregation framework combining partial parameter sharing and distillation—enabling faster convergence and resource-efficient federated learning on heterogeneous, non-IID data.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating client-side adaptive model pruning driven by stable, dynamically estimated local data heterogeneity metrics improves federated learning accuracy and communication efficiency on heterogeneous non-IID datasets beyond uniform global pruning strategies.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning with non-IID and heterogeneous client data suffers from degraded model accuracy and inefficient communication due to divergent local updates and overfitting on skewed client distributions. Existing pruning approaches apply uniform global compression, disregarding client-specific data heterogeneity, which may limit both model generalization and communication savings. A principled, adaptive pruning method that tailors model sparsity to local data characteristics is needed to better balance accuracy and communication cost in realistic federated settings.

APPROACH: We propose a federated learning framework where each client dynamically estimates its local data heterogeneity via stable metrics (e.g., smoothed label entropy and feature distribution divergence) aggregated over recent communication rounds to mitigate noise. Based on these heterogeneity estimates, structured channel pruning ratios for convolutional layers of a shared ResNet-18 model are adaptively assigned per client and per round, with more aggressive pruning for highly heterogeneous clients to reduce overfitting and communication load. We theoretically analyze the impact of heterogeneous pruning masks on FedAvg aggregation and empirically validate convergence stability, integrating pruning metadata communication costs into a refined communication budget metric.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on two representative non-IID benchmarks: CIFAR-10 partitioned by Dirichlet (α=0.5) to simulate synthetic label skew, and FEMNIST representing realistic cross-device heterogeneity. Baselines include No Pruning (Full Model) and Uniform Global Pruning (UGP) at multiple fixed pruning ratios. Metrics are final global test accuracy after 200 (CIFAR-10) or 50 (FEMNIST) rounds and cumulative communication cost counting both model weights and pruning metadata. We verify statistical significance with paired t-tests over 5 runs (p < 0.05) and perform ablations isolating pruning adaptivity versus hyperparameter effects. The hypothesis is supported if adaptive pruning outperforms the best UGP baseline by ≥2% accuracy at similar communication or matches accuracy with ≥20% communication savings, and is falsified otherwise.

NOVELTY: This work uniquely combines rigorously stabilized client-side heterogeneity estimation with dynamic, per-round structured pruning and a theoretical framework analyzing heterogeneous pruning effects on federated aggregation and convergence. Unlike prior adaptive pruning or heterogeneity-aware FL methods, we explicitly integrate pruning overhead into communication metrics and isolate the benefits of dynamic pruning adaptivity over fixed client-specific schedules, providing novel insights and practical guidelines for heterogeneous non-IID federated learning.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA: Incorporating client-side synthetic data augmentation via lightweight generative models mitigates statistical heterogeneity and client drift, improving global model accuracy and convergence speed in federated learning with heterogeneous, non-IID data beyond state-of-the-art personalized distillation methods.

BACKGROUND: Federated learning (FL) with heterogeneous and non-IID client data suffers from degraded global model accuracy and slow convergence due to client drift and statistical heterogeneity. While personalized distillation methods like FedGKD and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation alleviate these issues by knowledge fusion, they do not explicitly enrich local data diversity, limiting their effectiveness in highly skewed data scenarios.

APPROACH: We propose equipping each client with a lightweight generative model (e.g., conditional variational autoencoder or GAN) trained locally on its private data to generate synthetic samples that augment real data at a fixed ratio before local model training. This synthetic augmentation enhances local data diversity, reducing distributional skew without sharing raw data, and is combined with standard FedAvg aggregation, allowing the global model to better generalize across heterogeneous clients.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on the CIFAR-10 dataset partitioned into non-IID client distributions using a Dirichlet allocation with α=0.1, and the FEMNIST dataset which naturally exhibits heterogeneous, user-based data; all methods use an identical CNN backbone. Baselines include FedAvg (no augmentation), FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Key metrics are final global test accuracy averaged over clients after 200 communication rounds, convergence speed defined as rounds to reach 90% of final accuracy, and statistical significance assessed via multiple independent runs with confidence intervals and hypothesis testing. Ablations will systematically vary augmentation ratios and synthetic data quality, and practical overhead (computation, memory, communication) and training stability under client dropouts will also be measured.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior generative-model-based FL methods (e.g., FedGen, FedMix) and personalized distillation techniques, this work formally analyzes and empirically demonstrates that client-side synthetic data augmentation directly addresses statistical heterogeneity by enriching local data distributions, leading to significantly improved global accuracy (+3%+ over best baselines) and faster convergence, while rigorously quantifying trade-offs in overhead and stability under realistic heterogeneous and resource-constrained federated settings.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators trained on rigorously harmonized multi-model regional climate model ensembles will significantly improve out-of-sample prediction of extreme temperature and precipitation event frequency and intensity over Europe compared to single-model-trained emulators.

BACKGROUND:  
Simulating extreme regional climate events remains a critical challenge for accelerating climate projections using machine learning, especially due to limited training data and model structural biases. Existing deep learning emulators trained on single RCM-GCM pairs often fail to robustly capture extremes or generalize across temporal and spatial regimes. Leveraging multi-model ensembles promises to increase structural diversity and sample variability, but the benefits for extreme event emulation remain unproven and confounded by data heterogeneity.

APPROACH:  
We will develop two deep learning emulators with identical architectures (e.g., convolutional LSTM with uncertainty quantification via Monte Carlo dropout) trained on (1) a single EURO-CORDEX RCM-GCM pair (RCA4 driven by MPI-ESM1.2-LR) and (2) a harmonized, bias-corrected multi-model dataset combining at least five distinct RCM-GCM pairs with diverse physical parameterizations. Both will predict regional-scale extreme event statistics (block maxima and peak-over-threshold metrics for temperature and precipitation) conditioned on large-scale GCM predictors over 1980-2010, with explicit control baselines to isolate multi-model diversity effects.

EXPERIMENT:  
Models will be evaluated on held-out 2011-2020 EURO-CORDEX data and validated against the E-OBS observational dataset using spatially and temporally consistent definitions of extremes. Success criteria require the multi-model-trained emulator to achieve at least a 15% relative reduction in RMSE for out-of-sample extreme event frequency (exceedances above the 95th percentile) and intensity (magnitude of the 99th percentile) aggregated over multiple European subregions and seasons, with statistical significance (p < 0.05) established via bootstrap resampling and spatial-temporal dependence-aware metrics. Ablation studies controlling for data volume and bias correction will confirm performance gains arise from multi-model diversity rather than confounding factors.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines rigorous multi-model data harmonization and uncertainty-aware deep learning emulation focused explicitly on extremes, grounded in a theoretically motivated hypothesis linking structural model diversity to improved robustness in extreme event simulation. Unlike prior studies emphasizing climatological means or single-model emulators, this approach systematically quantifies multi-model diversity benefits with novel evaluation protocols that account for spatial-temporal dependence and rare event statistics, providing actionable insights for rapid, reliable regional climate risk assessment.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Training deep learning emulators on carefully balanced multi-model ensembles with domain-adversarial learning yields significantly improved generalization to unseen climate regimes compared to single-model trained emulators, as demonstrated by reduced prediction errors in out-of-sample and abrupt climate scenarios.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate and efficient climate model emulation is critical for enabling large ensemble experiments and high-resolution projections, but existing emulators often struggle to generalize beyond the trained climate states. Prior work typically trains emulators on single-model data or fails to rigorously isolate the benefits of multi-model diversity, limiting their ability to extrapolate to novel forcing scenarios and climate regimes. There is a pressing need to develop and validate emulators that robustly leverage multi-model variability to improve out-of-distribution predictive skill.

APPROACH:  
We propose to train a deep convolutional encoder-decoder network with residual connections and integrated domain-adversarial training on a balanced multi-model dataset combining outputs from at least five distinct CMIP6 GCMs, explicitly controlling for total training sample size to isolate model diversity effects. Single-model emulators with identical architectures will serve as baselines, and hyperparameter tuning and early stopping will be performed using validation sets representing future and abrupt climate scenarios to optimize true out-of-distribution performance.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate emulator generalization on held-out SSP2-4.5 scenario data across all models and the abrupt 4xCO2 ScenarioMIP run, measuring normalized root mean squared error (NRMSE) and spatial pattern correlation for surface temperature and precipitation, with statistical testing (paired t-tests, p < 0.05) to confirm at least 10% relative error reduction for multi-model emulators versus single-model baselines and classical linear pattern scaling.

NOVELTY:  
This study rigorously disentangles the effects of multi-model diversity from dataset size and explicitly incorporates domain-adversarial learning to extract domain-invariant features, advancing beyond prior work that lacks systematic control or theoretical grounding for multi-model generalization. It provides the first statistically robust evidence quantifying the practical benefits and limitations of multi-model deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration under realistic out-of-distribution conditions.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators for GCM-to-RCM seasonal downscaling that explicitly incorporate dynamic atmospheric teleconnection indices as input features will significantly improve regional temperature and precipitation prediction accuracy and robustness across multiple North American seasons compared to standard GCM-only emulators and classical downscaling methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Regional climate downscaling remains computationally expensive, and deep learning emulators offer a promising acceleration pathway but often underperform or lack robustness across seasons due to ignoring key climate teleconnections. While raw GCM fields contain implicit teleconnection signals, their spatiotemporal complexity and non-stationarity challenge emulators’ ability to extract predictive patterns, limiting multi-seasonal skill and transferability in regional climate prediction.

APPROACH:  
We propose augmenting a temporal convolutional network with attention mechanisms by explicitly including lagged and concurrent seasonal teleconnection indices (ENSO Niño 3.4, NAO, PDO) alongside large-scale GCM predictors, using domain adaptation techniques to address temporal non-stationarity in teleconnection-climate relationships. Rigorous ablation experiments—with matched model capacity and randomized teleconnection inputs—will isolate the added predictive value of teleconnection features, trained and tested on CORDEX North America RCM outputs driven by CMIP6 GCMs with ERA5-based validation.

EXPERIMENT:  
The emulator will be evaluated on independent 2011–2020 seasonal datasets (DJF, MAM, JJA) using spatial root mean squared error (RMSE) and spatial pattern correlation against ERA5 reanalysis as ground truth, compared to (1) a CNN-based GCM-only emulator, (2) a linear pattern scaling model, and (3) a persistence baseline. Success is defined as achieving at least 5% RMSE reduction and 0.03 correlation increase relative to the best baseline in both temperature and precipitation predictions consistently across at least three seasons, with statistical significance assessed via bootstrap confidence intervals and tests for covariate shift.

NOVELTY:  
Our approach uniquely combines explicit, dynamically lagged teleconnection indices with deep temporal architectures and domain adaptation to rigorously address temporal non-stationarity and isolate teleconnection contributions, advancing beyond prior work that relied solely on implicit GCM fields without robust validation frameworks or ablation controls for teleconnection signal efficacy in multi-seasonal regional climate downscaling emulators.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Deep learning emulators incorporating spatiotemporal attention mechanisms with physics-informed inductive biases will more accurately capture the frequency, intensity, and propagation of extreme climate events in EURO-CORDEX regional climate model outputs than traditional convolutional neural networks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for regional climate models (RCMs) often struggle to reliably represent extreme climate events, which are localized, nonlinear, and dynamically complex phenomena critical for impact assessments. Traditional CNN-based emulators emphasize local spatial features but have limited capacity to model long-range dependencies in space and time that govern extreme event evolution. Addressing the challenge of accurately emulating extremes with robust generalization across future climate conditions remains an open problem in climate model acceleration.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid deep learning emulator combining convolutional layers for local feature extraction with spatiotemporal multi-head self-attention mechanisms adapted from Transformer architectures to jointly model spatial and temporal dependencies on gridded climate data. Inputs consist of coarse-scale GCM fields (ERA5 reanalysis and CMIP6 historical and RCP8.5 projections) encoded with separate positional embeddings for space and time, while outputs are high-resolution daily EURO-CORDEX temperature and precipitation fields. The model is trained using a composite loss function emphasizing extreme event prediction fidelity (focal loss on extremes plus mean squared error), with ablation studies controlling for model capacity and data volume.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate performance on the EURO-CORDEX domain from 1971–2100, using temporally disjoint training (1971–2000) and out-of-sample testing on future periods (2001–2100) to rigorously assess generalization to unseen extreme event types and intensities. Metrics focus on extreme event characterization: Extreme Event Detection F1-score (exceedances above localized 95th percentiles), Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS) on extreme quantiles, and spatial pattern correlation of extreme event frequency maps compared to RCM ground truth. Success requires the attention-based model to outperform a ResNet-based 3D CNN baseline by at least 10% relative improvement in F1-score with statistical significance (p < 0.05, paired t-test) and demonstrate physically meaningful gains in capturing compound and multi-day extremes.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates spatiotemporal attention with convolutional feature extractors specifically designed to address the nonlinear propagation and spatial heterogeneity of extreme climate events in RCM emulation. Unlike prior Transformer-based climate models focused on mean climate statistics, we emphasize extremes with tailored loss functions, physics-informed positional embeddings, and out-of-sample extreme event validation under future climate scenarios, advancing the state of the art in climate model acceleration and extreme event representation.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and rigorously evaluate deep learning climate emulators trained with physically-informed composite loss functions to improve the long-term stability and physical fidelity of multi-decadal climate projections compared to purely data-driven models.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate models often prioritize statistical fit using data-driven loss functions, risking long-term instability and physically inconsistent projections over multi-decadal horizons. Incorporating physics-based constraints such as energy and mass conservation into training objectives could enhance emulator reliability, but systematic validation and theoretical justification for these benefits remain limited. Addressing this gap is critical to accelerate climate simulations without sacrificing physical realism or projection stability.

APPROACH:  
We propose training two variants of a U-Net convolutional neural network emulator on CMIP6 global climate model outputs (MPI-ESM1.2-LR and HadGEM3-GC31-LL) combined with EURO-CORDEX regional climate model data for downscaling. The physically-informed emulator optimizes a composite loss function blending mean squared error (MSE) with penalties enforcing conservation of energy (TOA net radiation balance) and precipitation water budget closure, while the baseline model optimizes MSE alone. We systematically explore hyperparameters, physical loss weights, and training robustness through cross-validation and multiple random seeds to isolate the impact of physics-aware training on emulator stability.

EXPERIMENT:  
Training occurs on 1950–2005 data; testing uses 2006–2100 multi-decadal projections under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 scenarios. Evaluation metrics include RMSE and Structural Similarity Index Measure (SSIM) against CORDEX outputs, long-term energy imbalance (absolute TOA radiation difference), precipitation water budget closure error, and temporal autocorrelation of key climate variables to detect drift. Success requires the physically-informed model to reduce RMSE by ≥10%, improve energy budget closure by ≥15%, and maintain temporal autocorrelation within 5% of ground truth, with falsification triggered if the baseline outperforms on these key metrics.

NOVELTY:  
This work provides a theoretically motivated and quantitatively rigorous framework for integrating physically-informed loss functions in climate emulators, explicitly validating their impact on long-term projection stability and physical consistency at both global and regional scales. Unlike prior studies that focus on short-term accuracy or downscaling, we systematically dissect physical constraint contributions with intermediate validation steps and multi-seed robustness tests, offering unmatched insight into the trade-offs and benefits of physics-aware deep learning emulation.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporating adversarial perturbations during training of deep neural networks improves uncertainty calibration under distributional shifts more effectively than probabilistic skip connections and established uncertainty quantification baselines.

BACKGROUND: Reliable uncertainty quantification (UQ) under natural distributional shifts remains a critical challenge for deploying deep neural networks in safety-critical applications. While methods like probabilistic skip connections (PSC) leverage intermediate feature distributions for deterministic UQ, their efficacy under realistic corruptions and out-of-distribution (OOD) data is often limited. Adversarial training is known to enhance model robustness, but its impact on improving calibrated uncertainty estimates under natural shifts has not been rigorously compared to specialized UQ techniques.

APPROACH: We propose to integrate adversarial training using projected gradient descent (PGD) perturbations at ε=8/255 (L∞ norm, 7 steps per batch) into the training of ResNet-50 models, optimizing a combined loss on clean and adversarial samples. Post-training, temperature scaling calibrated on a clean validation set will be applied to enable fair calibration comparisons with PSC and other UQ baselines (deep ensembles, MC Dropout, temperature scaling alone). This design isolates the effect of adversarial robustness on uncertainty calibration under distributional shifts.

EXPERIMENT: Models will be trained on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 and evaluated on their corrupted variants CIFAR-10-C and CIFAR-100-C across multiple corruption types and severity levels, measuring Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) using a weighted aggregation scheme that accounts for corruption severity frequency. OOD detection performance will be assessed on SVHN. Statistical significance will be established via confidence intervals and hypothesis testing over multiple independent runs. The hypothesis will be supported if the adversarially trained model achieves a ≥20% relative improvement in ECE and NLL over PSC and other baselines averaged across shifts; falsified if PSC or baselines match or exceed adversarial training within 5%.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously evaluates adversarial training’s impact on uncertainty calibration under realistic natural corruptions, contrasting it with recent deterministic UQ methods like PSC and classical baselines, supported by statistically robust analysis. It also theoretically elucidates why enforcing local robustness via adversarial perturbations can yield better calibrated predictive uncertainties under distributional shifts, advancing beyond existing robust calibration literature.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Integrating structured domain knowledge as differentiable soft constraints within the latent space of constrained variational autoencoders reduces predictive uncertainty and improves out-of-distribution detection under distributional shift in medical image diagnosis more effectively than purely data-driven latent-space uncertainty quantification methods.

BACKGROUND: Accurate uncertainty quantification (UQ) in deep neural networks remains a critical challenge, especially under realistic distributional shifts such as changes in patient populations and imaging protocols common in clinical settings. Existing latent-space UQ approaches rely solely on data-driven representations, which often fail to capture known physiological relationships and disease co-occurrences, limiting robustness and calibration when deployed out-of-distribution.

APPROACH: We propose a constrained variational autoencoder (CVAE) architecture that incorporates clinically validated domain knowledge graphs—encoding anatomical constraints and disease co-morbidity patterns—as differentiable regularizers \(\mathcal{L}_c(z)\) on the latent code \(z\). These constraints are weighted and balanced against reconstruction and KL losses to avoid latent collapse, with ablation studies guiding hyperparameter selection and ensuring stable training. Predictive uncertainty is then quantified via entropy and mutual information computed from the constrained latent posterior distributions.

EXPERIMENT: We train models on the ChestX-ray14 dataset and evaluate under distributional shift on the MIMIC-CXR dataset, measuring uncertainty quality with multilabel Expected Calibration Error (ECE) using class-wise and macro-averaged binning, negative log-likelihood (NLL), and out-of-distribution detection AUROC for distinguishing ChestX-ray14 from MIMIC-CXR samples. Baselines include the unconstrained latent-space UQ method from *Uncertainty Quantification in Deep Neural Networks through Statistical Inference on Latent Space (2023)*, Monte Carlo Dropout, unconstrained VAEs, and CVAEs with randomized or ablated constraints. Statistical significance is assessed via multiple training runs, reporting 95% confidence intervals and applying paired tests to confirm at least 10% relative improvements in ECE/NLL and 5% in AUROC under shift.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior latent-space UQ models and knowledge-regularized representations that either use static post-hoc corrections or heuristic domain incorporations, our method tightly integrates structured, clinically validated domain knowledge as soft constraints directly within the latent representation learning process, theoretically promoting latent disentanglement aligned with physiological priors. This principled fusion is specifically designed and validated to improve predictive uncertainty calibration and OOD detection robustness under clinically relevant distributional changes.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporating domain-specific physical uncertainty priors into the latent space of deep neural networks improves uncertainty calibration and out-of-distribution robustness beyond standard Bayesian methods in climate modeling prediction tasks.

BACKGROUND: Accurately quantifying uncertainty in deep neural networks is critical for high-stakes applications like climate modeling, where predictions often encounter out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios due to changing environmental conditions. Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods, such as Monte Carlo Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and Bayesian Neural Networks, typically operate without explicit incorporation of physical knowledge, limiting their calibration and reliability on OOD data where physical constraints remain valid but data distributions shift.

APPROACH: We embed physically informed uncertainty priors—derived from domain knowledge such as physically plausible correlations among climate variables—into the latent space of a deep neural network using a conditional normalizing flow model. The network is trained with a combined loss that regularizes latent representations to match these priors via KL divergence alongside the standard prediction loss, thereby biasing uncertainty estimates towards physically consistent structures. At inference, uncertainty is derived from posterior latent samples under this physically constrained latent distribution, contrasting with parameter-space Bayesian methods that lack explicit physical guidance.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on the ClimateBench dataset, generating physically consistent OOD test sets by perturbing key climate parameters (e.g., greenhouse gas forcing, ocean circulation) following a rigorously validated, reproducible protocol ensuring stable ground truth labels. Calibration performance is assessed via Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and Negative Log Likelihood (NLL), with success defined as ≥20% improvement over Monte Carlo Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and variational Bayesian Neural Network baselines. OOD detection is measured by AUROC on uncertainty scores, requiring ≥10% gains over baselines. Additional experiments include in-distribution calibration checks, ablation studies isolating latent prior effects, and training stability analyses to ensure reproducibility and isolate causal impact.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior physics-informed UQ methods that impose constraints in parameter space or output distributions, this approach uniquely integrates physical uncertainty priors directly into the latent space via normalizing flows, providing a theoretically motivated and empirically validated pathway for improved calibration and OOD robustness. This method also distinguishes itself through rigorous OOD scenario generation grounded in climate physics and comprehensive ablations confirming the specific benefit of latent-space physical priors.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating learned hierarchical latent variables to propagate uncertainty within deep neural networks will yield significantly better-calibrated uncertainty estimates and enhanced out-of-distribution detection than Monte Carlo Dropout, deep ensembles, and variational Bayesian neural networks on high-dimensional real-world vision and time-series datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Reliable uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks remains challenging, especially in complex, high-dimensional settings where existing methods like Monte Carlo Dropout and ensembles either underestimate uncertainty or entail prohibitive computational costs. Current approaches often fail to capture structured epistemic and aleatoric uncertainties simultaneously across multiple feature abstraction levels, limiting their calibration and robustness under distributional shift.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical latent variable framework integrated into standard architectures (e.g., ResNet for vision, recurrent networks for time-series) by inserting conditional Gaussian latent variables after key layers, whose parameters are learned via amortized variational inference. This enables uncertainty propagation through multiple representation levels during both training and inference, explicitly modeling uncertainty decomposition and hierarchical feature uncertainty. Training optimizes a combined negative log-likelihood and KL divergence loss, ensuring a discriminative yet probabilistically principled model.

EXPERIMENT:  
We quantitatively evaluate on ImageNet-1k (for vision) and PhysioNet Challenge 2019 (for healthcare time-series), benchmarking against Monte Carlo Dropout (50 stochastic passes), deep ensembles (5 models), and variational Bayesian neural networks. Metrics include Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL), Brier score, and AUROC for OOD detection using ImageNet-C and MIMIC-III as OOD sets. Success requires ≥20% relative improvements in ECE and NLL and statistically significant (p < 0.01) AUROC gains ≥0.05 over all baselines; failure is declared if improvements fall below 5% or AUROC gains under 0.02. Ablations disentangle epistemic/aleatoric uncertainty, and computational experiments assess scalability with explicit hardware and training time considerations.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work focusing on single-layer latent uncertainty or parameter subspaces, our hierarchical latent variable model explicitly structures uncertainty propagation across multiple feature levels, providing a theoretically grounded decomposition of uncertainty sources that improves calibration and OOD detection. This approach addresses posterior collapse and variational limitations through architecture-informed latent conditioning and comprehensive ablations, distinguishing it from recent latent space or probabilistic skip connection methods and ensemble approximations.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Incorporate an adaptive temporal consistency constraint with a learned gating mechanism into sequential deep neural networks to improve uncertainty calibration on smoothly evolving time series while dynamically relaxing constraints to avoid miscalibration in non-stationary environments.

BACKGROUND: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) in sequential deep neural networks faces challenges when temporal dynamics vary from smooth to abrupt regimes, causing standard temporal smoothing to either under- or over-regularize uncertainty estimates. Existing methods typically apply fixed temporal regularization or Bayesian inference without adaptively accounting for temporal heterogeneity, limiting calibration performance across diverse real-world time series.

APPROACH: We propose augmenting an LSTM/GRU-based sequential model with a temporal uncertainty quantification module trained under a temporal consistency loss that penalizes abrupt uncertainty changes across time steps. Crucially, this penalty is modulated by a learned gating mechanism—a temporal change detector—that dynamically adjusts constraint strength by identifying abrupt uncertainty transitions, allowing the model to adaptively enforce smoothness only when appropriate.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on two contrasting real-world datasets: the PhysioNet Sepsis Prediction dataset with smooth physiological signals, and the Electricity Load Diagrams (ELD) dataset featuring non-stationary demand and abrupt regime shifts. Metrics include temporally resolved Expected Calibration Error (ECE) computed per timestep across sequences using fixed-width binning and aggregated by weighted averaging, along with Negative Log Likelihood (NLL) and Brier scores. Baselines are (1) vanilla LSTM with Monte Carlo Dropout, (2) LSTM plus fixed temporal consistency regularization, and (3) state-of-the-art Bayesian RNN methods (e.g., variational LSTM). We perform hyperparameter sweeps over gating sensitivity and strength, include heuristic gating baselines, and conduct statistical significance tests over 5 random seeds. Success is defined as ≥10% relative reduction in temporal ECE versus fixed smoothing on PhysioNet without >5% degradation on ELD, falsifying the hypothesis if these criteria fail.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that applies fixed temporal smoothing or purely Bayesian sequential models, our method explicitly models adaptive temporal uncertainty dynamics via a learned gating mechanism, bridging uncertainty quantification with changepoint detection. This principled integration of temporal consistency constraints and adaptive relaxation tailored to temporal heterogeneity is novel and rigorously evaluated with a new, clearly defined temporal calibration metric and comprehensive ablations.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Embedding epigenetic chromatin accessibility data alongside transcriptomic profiles in a unified multimodal transformer foundation model with cross-attention layers will improve cell-type classification accuracy in single-cell genomics by 5-10% compared to transcriptomics-only models with matched capacity.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate cell-type classification from single-cell data is critical for understanding cellular heterogeneity and function. While transcriptomics provides a primary view of gene expression, epigenetic data such as chromatin accessibility captures complementary regulatory information that is often noisy and sparse. Prior efforts have integrated multi-omic single-cell data via simple concatenation or separate models, but the extent to which joint multimodal foundation models can leverage complementary signals to meaningfully improve classification remains unclear and underexplored.

APPROACH:  
We propose training a multimodal transformer-based foundation model with modality-specific encoders for RNA-seq and ATAC-seq data from the same cells, connected via cross-attention layers to learn integrated, context-aware embeddings. The model will be trained with a multi-task objective combining modality reconstruction and cell-type classification, designed to encourage robust joint representations. Rigorous hyperparameter tuning and early stopping will ensure fair comparison to single-modal baselines matched in parameter count as well as a simpler baseline concatenation model.

EXPERIMENT:  
Using paired single-cell multi-omic datasets SHARE-seq (mouse skin) and SNARE-seq (mouse brain), we will evaluate cell-type classification accuracy on held-out test sets. Models include: (1) RNA-only transformer baseline; (2) ATAC-only transformer baseline; (3) multimodal transformer with cross-attention; and (4) multimodal concatenation baseline. Success is defined as the multimodal cross-attention model achieving a consistent 5-10% absolute accuracy improvement over the RNA-only baseline, with improvements under 3% considered negligible.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines modality-specific transformer encoders with cross-attention layers and a multi-task loss to integrate multi-omic single-cell data, providing a theoretically grounded framework that moves beyond existing unimodal or simple concatenation approaches. By explicitly benchmarking parameter-matched controls and carefully setting realistic performance targets informed by prior benchmarks, it offers a more rigorous assessment of the true added value of integrated epigenetic information in foundation models for single-cell biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Jointly pretrained single-cell foundation models on integrated multi-organ, multi-species datasets achieve superior rare cell state prediction compared to organ- or species-specific models, validated by rigorous cross-validation on independent rare cell type datasets.

BACKGROUND: Accurately identifying rare cell states from single-cell RNA-seq data remains challenging due to limited labeled examples and biological heterogeneity. Existing single-cell foundation models typically focus on either single organs or species, limiting their generalizability and transferability to rare or understudied cell populations. Integrating multi-organ and multi-species single-cell data in foundation models may enhance representation learning, but the feasibility and benefits of such joint pretraining remain unexplored.

APPROACH: We will develop transformer-based single-cell foundation models pretrained using masked gene expression and contrastive learning objectives on harmonized human and mouse multi-organ datasets (Tabula Sapiens and Tabula Muris Senis), with rigorous batch correction, gene orthology mapping, and annotation standardization to enable stable joint training. Comparative models pretrained solely on organ-specific (kidney or lung) or species-specific (human or mouse) data will serve as baselines. All models will be fine-tuned on limited labeled data (5%) from independent rare cell datasets and evaluated on held-out rare cell populations.

EXPERIMENT: Performance will be assessed on predicting rare cell states in the Human Cell Atlas bone marrow dataset and the Mouse Kidney Cell Atlas using macro-averaged F1-score and precision-recall AUC. Statistical significance of at least 5 percentage points improvement in F1-score by the joint multi-organ, multi-species model over baselines will be tested via paired bootstrap tests. Additional ablations with size-matched training sets will isolate effects of data diversity from dataset scale.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior scFMs focusing on single organs or species, this work uniquely integrates multi-organ, cross-species single-cell data through explicit harmonization and orthology-aware modeling, systematically demonstrating improved rare cell state prediction and biological transferability—addressing a critical gap in foundation model generalizability and practical utility in single-cell genomics.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA: Integrating epigenomic (scATAC-seq) and spatial transcriptomic data into transformer-based single-cell foundation models via modality-specific contrastive encoders and cross-attention fusion will significantly improve cell type classification accuracy over transcriptomics-only models across diverse multi-omic benchmarks.

BACKGROUND: Current single-cell foundation models predominantly rely on transcriptomic data, limiting their ability to fully capture complex cellular identity shaped by chromatin accessibility and spatial context. Integrating multi-omic modalities remains challenging due to modality-specific noise, alignment ambiguities, and limited generalization across tissues and species, leaving open the question of whether multi-modal scFMs yield robust, generalizable improvements in cell type classification.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-modal scFM architecture that first generates latent embeddings for scRNA-seq, scATAC-seq, and spatial transcriptomics via modality-specific encoders pretrained with contrastive learning to capture modality-intrinsic features. These embeddings are then fused through cross-attention layers to learn joint representations, allowing the model to leverage complementary biological signals. We will conduct intermediate ablation studies (e.g., transcriptomics + scATAC-seq, transcriptomics + spatial only) to dissect each modality’s contribution and apply rigorous preprocessing and cell-to-spot mapping strategies to address alignment challenges.

EXPERIMENT: Using multi-omic Human Cell Atlas (HCA) brain cortex datasets (10x Genomics Multiome and spatial Visium) for training and cross-validation, and independent Mouse Cell Atlas (MCA) multi-omic data for external validation, we will compare classification accuracy and macro F1-score against transcriptomics-only baselines (scBERT, scVI). Success is defined as a statistically significant (p < 0.01) ≥3–5% absolute improvement in both metrics consistently across datasets and splits, with paired t-tests and power analysis ensuring robustness.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior single-cell models that either focus solely on transcriptomics or integrate modalities via simple concatenation or co-embedding, our model leverages contrastive pretraining for each modality and cross-attention fusion to capture complex cross-modal interactions explicitly. This theoretically grounded architecture, combined with rigorous ablation and cross-species validation, uniquely addresses modality-specific challenges to yield interpretable, generalizable multi-omic foundation models for single-cell biology.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and rigorously evaluate a multimodal foundation model integrating single-cell ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, and spatial transcriptomics data using transformer and graph neural network architectures to improve prediction of cell-type-specific regulatory networks beyond unimodal approaches.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate inference of cell-type-specific regulatory networks is critical for understanding gene regulation in complex tissues, yet current foundation models typically rely on single modalities such as transcriptomics alone, limiting their ability to capture the full regulatory landscape. Multi-omic single-cell datasets combining epigenomic, transcriptomic, and spatial data offer complementary information but present challenges in effective integration and noise management. There is a lack of rigorous benchmarks and theoretical justification demonstrating that multimodal integration yields significant predictive improvements over strong unimodal baselines in single-cell regulatory network inference.

APPROACH:  
We propose a transformer-based multimodal foundation model with dedicated encoders for ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, and spatial transcriptomics modalities, followed by a joint latent space fusion module employing cross-attention layers to explicitly model cross-modal interactions. Spatial neighborhood relationships will be captured using graph neural networks applied to cells’ spatial coordinates, enabling context-aware embedding refinement. Ablation studies will compare against unimodal models trained with matched parameter budgets, early concatenation integration, and best-in-class transcriptomic-only models (e.g., scBERT, CellFormer), to isolate the benefit of multimodal fusion.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be performed on publicly available multi-omic single-cell atlases including human lung and brain datasets from the Human Cell Atlas with matched single-cell ATAC-seq, RNA-seq, and spatial transcriptomics, as well as the Mouse Cell Atlas for cross-species validation. Performance will be assessed using area under the precision-recall curve (AUPR) for predicting validated cell-type-specific TF-target regulatory interactions curated from ENCODE and single-cell regulatory network databases, employing 5-fold tissue-level cross-validation. Secondary metrics include cell-type classification accuracy and spatial neighborhood concordance measured by adjusted Rand index (ARI). Statistical significance will be tested via paired tests across folds; failure to improve AUPR by ≥10% over best unimodal baselines with p ≤ 0.05 will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work provides a formal theoretical rationale and empirically tested architecture explicitly designed to model cross-modal and spatial interactions in single-cell regulatory inference, going beyond prior unimodal or simplistic early-integration approaches. By incorporating graph neural networks for spatial contexts and rigorous ablation controls, this study sets a new standard for evaluating multimodal foundation models in genomics, addressing noise and data heterogeneity challenges not fully explored in existing models such as scBERT, CellFormer, or Nephrobase Cell+.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Foundation models pretrained on multimodal single-cell data integrating transcriptomics and epigenomics via cross-modal transformer architectures outperform unimodal models in predicting cell-type-specific regulatory elements across diverse human and mouse tissues.

BACKGROUND:  
Accurate identification of cell-type-specific regulatory elements is critical for understanding gene regulation but remains challenging due to the complex interplay between transcriptional output and chromatin state. Existing unimodal models trained solely on either single-cell transcriptomic or epigenomic data lack the integrated context necessary to robustly capture regulatory grammar across varied tissues and species. Multimodal integration has potential to improve prediction sensitivity and generalization but requires rigorous validation and careful architectural design.

APPROACH:  
We propose a dual-stream transformer foundation model that jointly encodes scRNA-seq gene expression and scATAC-seq peak accessibility from matched single cells using modality-specific encoders and cross-modal attention layers to learn shared latent representations. Pretraining employs masked gene expression prediction and peak accessibility imputation to capture cross-modal dependencies, followed by fine-tuning on enhancer activity classification with experimentally validated cell-type-specific labels. Rigorous ablation studies will compare fusion strategies and unimodal baselines trained under identical protocols to isolate multimodal contributions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train and evaluate models on the Human Cell Atlas multi-omics reference dataset (paired scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq across immune, epithelial, and stromal human cells) and the SHARE-seq mouse skin dataset for cross-species validation. Performance will be benchmarked using area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC) on held-out tissues and cell types from ENCODE and FANTOM5 enhancer activity datasets. Controls include unimodal transcriptomic-only (e.g., scBERT) and epigenomic-only (e.g., scATAC-CLIP) models pretrained identically, with success defined as multimodal AUPRC improvements ≥0.05 over best unimodal baselines and failure defined as ≤0.02 improvement or worse. Cross-tissue and cross-species generalization will be assessed with domain adaptation and orthology mapping strategies.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines dual-stream cross-modal transformers with masked modeling objectives tailored for single-cell multimodal data, providing mechanistic insight into integrated regulatory grammar. Unlike prior foundation models focused on single modalities or single tissues, our controlled comparison rigorously isolates the value of multimodal integration across species and diverse tissue types, addressing modality noise imbalance and biological interpretability challenges through explicit validation and ablation analyses.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Introducing adaptive temporal variability in discrete communication timing improves the robustness of emergent multi-agent communication protocols against structured adversarial noise by reducing temporal predictability and redundancy.

BACKGROUND: Robust and efficient communication protocols are critical for coordination in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) systems, especially under noisy or adversarial conditions. While prior work has explored message content encoding and sparse signaling, it remains an open question whether temporal modulation of communication timing—beyond fixed-interval or sparsity-biased schemes—can enhance noise resilience by leveraging principles from information theory and coding under channel uncertainty.

APPROACH: We propose augmenting MARL agents with a learnable temporal gating mechanism—a Bernoulli latent variable conditioned on agent observations and past messages—that adaptively modulates when discrete messages are emitted, maintaining matched average communication rates across conditions. This mechanism induces temporal variability in message timing, hypothesized to mitigate noise effects by decorrelating transmissions and enabling implicit error correction through timing patterns. To isolate timing effects, adversarial noise is modeled as Gaussian perturbations applied to continuous message embeddings prior to discretization, reflecting realistic channel distortion with controlled signal-to-noise ratios.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate this hypothesis in the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) on the Cooperative Navigation task, training agents under two conditions—fixed-timing communication (messages every timestep) versus variable-timing communication with temporal gating—using MADDPG and an alternative MARL algorithm (e.g., QMIX) for generality. Performance is measured by average episode reward and communication mutual information across systematically varied noise levels injected during test time; statistical significance and confidence intervals over at least 10 random seeds will determine robustness gains. Ablations include fixed-timing agents matched for average communication rate and variable-timing agents without gating to confirm causal effects.

NOVELTY: This work is the first to rigorously test temporal variability in discrete emergent communication timing as a robustness mechanism grounded in information-theoretic principles, distinctly separating timing modulation from sparsity or encoding redundancy. The combination of temporal gating with matched communication rates and noise models tailored to discrete channels extends beyond prior studies focused on sparsity biases or content encoding, offering new insights into temporal coding strategies for noisy multi-agent systems.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Multi-agent systems trained under dynamically changing environmental pressures will naturally develop context-dependent, syntax-like communication protocols exhibiting hierarchical and compositional structure, in contrast to agents trained in static environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication research largely focuses on static or fixed-task environments, limiting the complexity and adaptive nature of learned protocols. However, real-world multi-agent systems often face evolving objectives and contexts demanding flexible, structured communication akin to human language syntax. Understanding whether and how dynamic environmental pressures induce such syntax-like structures remains an open problem critical for advancing robust, generalizable multi-agent coordination.

APPROACH:  
We propose to train multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) agents in the Google Research Football (GRF) environment extended with procedurally generated, dynamic changes in tasks, agent roles, and field conditions that vary at controlled intervals. Agents communicate via fixed-length discrete token sequences without explicit inductive biases, allowing communication protocols to evolve naturally under environmental complexity. We will rigorously disentangle the effects of environment dynamics and architectural biases through a series of controlled ablation studies, including static versus dynamic environments and presence versus absence of inductive communication biases.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate emergent communication using well-defined metrics: (1) conditional mutual information (MI) between message tokens and environmental contexts estimated via k-nearest neighbor entropy estimators with confidence intervals; (2) topographical similarity scores to quantify compositional mapping between messages and task components; and (3) zero-shot generalization accuracy on unseen task variants to assess syntactic communication utility. Statistical significance will be assessed using permutation tests and bootstrapped confidence intervals over multiple training seeds and episodes. Additional causal analyses—such as probing hierarchical message structures using sequence perturbation tests and disentangled representation metrics—will verify genuine syntax-like properties. Failure to observe significantly higher conditional MI (>0.6 bits), topographical similarity (>0.5), and >75% generalization accuracy in dynamic versus static groups will falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that studies emergent communication primarily in static settings or with hand-engineered inductive biases, this research uniquely investigates the causal role of dynamic environmental pressures in inducing hierarchical, context-dependent, and compositional communication protocols without explicit architectural constraints. By combining rigorous metric definitions, ablation controls, and causal structural analyses, it provides the first comprehensive demonstration of syntax-like communication emergence driven by evolving multi-agent environments.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA: Multi-agent systems that develop communication protocols constrained by biologically calibrated energy budgets will evolve more robust, generalizable, and semantically efficient languages than those trained solely for task performance without energy constraints.

BACKGROUND: Robust and generalizable emergent communication remains a critical challenge in multi-agent reinforcement learning, with existing methods often producing brittle or overly task-specific protocols. While biological systems operate under tight energy constraints shaping neural communication, current emergent communication research rarely incorporates such constraints, limiting insights into how energy budgeting might induce more transferable and efficient languages.

APPROACH: We propose training two matched groups of agents in the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MPE) cooperative navigation task: one with an additive energy regularization term grounded in neuroscientifically derived spike energy costs that penalizes communication based on bit-level energy consumption, and one unconstrained baseline optimizing task performance only. Energy regularization weight (\(\lambda\)) will be systematically calibrated via ablations to balance energy cost against task success, while emergent communication will be analyzed using richer metrics capturing semantic content, syntactic structure, and redundancy to disentangle effects of energy constraints on language properties.

EXPERIMENT: Agents will be evaluated on Task Transfer Success Rate (TTSR) in unseen MPE navigation scenarios with novel partner agents paired according to controlled partner-matching protocols, and Communication Efficiency (CE) measured as energy-weighted average bits per episode; baselines include Unconstrained Baseline Agents and Positive Signalling/Listening agents from (Cao et al., 2019). Statistical analyses with sufficient scale and variability controls will test whether energy-constrained agents yield at least 15% higher TTSR without exceeding a 10% CE increase, falsifying the hypothesis if not.

NOVELTY: This work rigorously grounds energy constraints in biologically plausible neural spike costs with principled hyperparameter tuning, integrates semantic and structural communication quality metrics beyond bit counts, and employs a robust transfer evaluation framework that isolates the impact of energy-budgeted communication on language robustness—advancing beyond prior inductive bias and bandwidth-efficiency studies by explicitly linking energy constraints to emergent language generalization in multi-agent systems.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Multi-agent systems trained with communication protocols evolving under realistic, temporally correlated wireless channel noise develop more robust, generalizable, and efficient emergent languages than those trained in noise-free environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Robust and generalizable emergent communication protocols remain a central challenge in multi-agent systems, especially under environmental noise that degrades message fidelity. While prior work explores inductive biases and simplified noise models, there is limited understanding of how realistic, temporally correlated wireless noise shapes the structure and resilience of emergent protocols. Addressing this gap is critical for deploying multi-agent systems in noisy, real-world communication settings such as urban microcells.

APPROACH:  
We extend the Multi-agent Particle Environment (MPE) Cooperative Navigation benchmark by integrating a validated, differentiable noise injection pipeline modeled on Wireless InSite urban microcell datasets, capturing packet loss, bit-flip errors, signal attenuation, and temporal noise correlations. Two groups of agents using CommNet architectures will be trained with and without noise-aware communication channels, alongside baselines including no communication, fixed-symbol communication, and architectures enhanced with explicit error-correcting codes. We analyze emergent protocol robustness via metrics including task completion, mutual information between messages and environment states, message entropy, degradation curves under varying noise, and communication efficiency.

EXPERIMENT:  
Agents will be trained for 1 million steps under varying noise levels and evaluated on in-distribution and out-of-distribution noise intensities (including higher noise than training). Statistical robustness will be ensured via 50+ evaluation runs and mixed-effects modeling. Quantitative falsification criteria require the noise-trained group to outperform noise-free agents by >5% absolute task completion (p<0.05, paired t-test) under novel noise, with lower message error rates, higher mutual information, and more compact messages; failure to meet these metrics falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work pioneers the integration of realistic, temporally correlated wireless noise in end-to-end differentiable emergent communication training, explicitly compares noise-aware learning against error-correcting architectures, and rigorously quantifies communication robustness through theoretically grounded metrics beyond task success. Unlike prior studies focusing on inductive biases or simplified noise, we characterize how environmental noise shapes emergent protocol structure and generalization bounds in multi-agent reinforcement learning.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Multi-agent systems trained with dynamically evolving, empirically grounded communication noise and equipped with inductive biases that explicitly promote robustness—such as noise-aware variational autoencoder-based message encoding and structured communication bottlenecks—will develop emergent communication protocols exhibiting increased redundancy and error correction compared to noiseless or static-noise baselines.

BACKGROUND:  
Emergent communication protocols in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) often degrade significantly under realistic environmental uncertainties like dynamic noise, limiting their applicability in real-world settings. Prior work has largely focused on static noise models or general positive signaling/listening biases without systematically examining how temporally varying noise—mimicking real wireless channels—shapes protocol evolution or how architectural inductive biases can robustly induce error-correcting codes. This gap hinders our understanding of how environmental uncertainty drives the functional complexity of emergent languages.

APPROACH:  
We propose to integrate a dynamically varying noise model based on empirical wireless noise characteristics (e.g., Rayleigh fading, burst errors) into the communication channel of MARL agents operating in the Google Research Football Environment, ensuring noise injection exclusively affects inter-agent messages without perturbing environment state dynamics. Agents will be augmented with a noise-aware variational autoencoder (VAE) that jointly learns to reconstruct corrupted messages, coupled with a sparsity-enforcing communication bottleneck to bias encoding toward redundancy and robustness. Ablation studies will systematically isolate the effects of noise dynamics, representational capacity, and inductive biases, controlling for model size and training duration.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on cooperative multi-agent navigation tasks from the GRFE dataset under partial observability. Baselines include (1) noiseless communication without inductive bias, (2) static noise with no bias, and (3) dynamic noise without specialized architectures. Metrics will extend beyond normalized mutual information (NMI) to include decoding error rates, minimum Hamming distances between message codewords, and task reward degradation under noise stress tests. Intended meanings will be annotated via environment state features and task goals to rigorously quantify protocol fidelity. The hypothesis is falsified if agents with dynamic noise and inductive biases do not exhibit statistically significant improvements (>10% NMI increase, >15% smaller task performance drop, p<0.05) relative to all baselines.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely models temporally evolving, empirically inspired communication noise to realistically mimic environmental uncertainty and couples it with a theoretically motivated noise-aware VAE inductive bias that explicitly encourages error-correcting emergent protocols. Unlike prior studies focused on static noise or generic positive signaling biases, our approach formally links dynamic noise exposure with the evolution of robust, redundant communication and rigorously disentangles robustness from overall policy competence through novel communication-centric metrics and controlled ablations.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Individual attention heads in multi-task fine-tuned transformer models encode distinct, non-overlapping semantic roles that can be causally isolated via rigorous, stable attribution and targeted ablations, yielding task-specific performance degradation without cross-task interference.

BACKGROUND:  
Despite advances in mechanistic interpretability, it remains unclear whether semantic roles are localized to individual attention heads or distributed redundantly across multiple heads in transformer models. Prior work often relies on noisy attribution scores and coarse ablations, leaving open whether causal isolation of semantic functions at the head level is feasible, especially in multi-task settings with clearly distinct linguistic operations.

APPROACH:  
We propose a robust protocol to identify “important” attention heads by combining multiple attribution methods—attention rollout, Integrated Gradients, and causal mediation analysis—with stability assessments across random seeds and input subsets. We then perform fine-grained, controlled ablations including single-head, multi-head (random and importance-matched), layer-wise, and context-dependent interventions on a T5-base model jointly fine-tuned on semantically distinct GLUE (MNLI) and SQuAD v2.0 tasks, monitoring per-task and per-class metrics as well as intermediate activations and confidence distributions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate task performance via MNLI accuracy and SQuAD F1 on held-out validation sets, measuring the isolated and cross-task effects of ablating heads identified as critical for one task. Success requires statistically significant (>5%) degradation restricted to the identified task with negligible (<1%) impact on the unrelated task, assessed via paired bootstrap tests over multiple seeds and input samples. Failure—falsifying the hypothesis—is indicated by consistent cross-task drops (>3%) upon single-head ablations or random ablations causing comparable interference.

NOVELTY:  
This study advances mechanistic interpretability by integrating stable, multi-method causal attribution with systematic, multi-granularity ablations in a multi-task transformer setting, rigorously testing the localization versus distribution of semantic roles at the attention head level—addressing gaps in robustness, causal isolation, and cross-task generality unexplored in prior work.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Investigate whether sparse linear combinations of subsets of attention head outputs in a pretrained GPT-2 XL can accurately reconstruct intermediate residual stream representations within bounded error on naturalistic language datasets, thereby assessing the degree of linear compositionality underlying mechanistic interpretability.

BACKGROUND:  
While prior work has shown that individual attention heads often learn interpretable functions, it remains unclear how these heads collectively contribute to intermediate representations, which may be nonlinear and distributed. Clarifying whether small subsets of heads can linearly approximate residual stream vectors would provide a principled proxy for understanding compositionality and disentanglement in transformer internals, addressing an open problem in mechanistic interpretability regarding the linearity of head contributions across contexts.

APPROACH:  
We extract residual stream activations immediately following the multi-head attention sublayer at selected middle layers (6, 9, and 12) of GPT-2 XL and attempt to reconstruct them as sparse linear combinations of the corresponding attention heads’ output vectors. Sparse subset selection will be performed using scalable greedy forward selection with cross-validation, combined with rigorous normalization of head outputs and residual vectors (e.g., layerwise standardization) to ensure comparable scales and valid error metrics. We will compare sparse linear models against baselines including full-head linear regression, randomly weighted subsets, mean head outputs, and nonlinear reconstructions via small MLPs trained on the same data.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate reconstruction quality on two naturalistic corpora—OpenWebText and WikiText-103—measuring normalized mean squared error (MSE relative to residual vector norms) averaged over tokens and batches. Stratified analyses by token frequency and syntactic category will assess contextual variation. The hypothesis is supported if sparse linear combinations with at most 10 heads per layer achieve normalized reconstruction error below 10% on both datasets, whereas failure to do so even with up to 24 heads or substantially better performance from nonlinear models (>50% relative error reduction) will falsify it.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior linear probing or singular vector decomposition methods that treat heads independently or focus on orthogonal directions, this work explicitly formulates sparse subset selection as a scalable, normalized sparse regression problem with principled cross-validation to quantify linear compositionality of head outputs in reconstructing residual streams. This approach bridges interpretability and sparse modeling with rigorous experimental controls, providing a refined lens on the nonlinear entanglement question in transformer mechanistic interpretability.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Functional components driving language generation in transformer models correspond predominantly to sparse nonlinear interactions between principal singular vectors of attention and MLP weight matrices, and targeted perturbations of these interactions cause disproportionately greater degradation in model performance than perturbations of entire heads, MLP blocks, or random neuron subsets.

BACKGROUND:  
While previous mechanistic interpretability studies focus on attention heads or MLP blocks as functional units, it remains unclear whether finer-grained, sparse nonlinear interactions within these components better explain language generation behavior. Identifying such sparse interactions could enable a more precise mechanistic understanding of transformer computations beyond coarse component-level analyses. This addresses the challenge of pinpointing interpretable, causally relevant substructures that drive model outputs.

APPROACH:  
We propose a rigorously defined, reproducible pipeline that first applies singular value decomposition (SVD) to the weight matrices of individual attention head projections and MLP layers in a pretrained GPT-2 medium model. Next, gradient-based attribution methods (e.g., integrated gradients) are used on validation data from Penn Treebank and WikiText-103 to identify sparse nonlinear interactions between small groups of principal singular vectors that disproportionately influence output logits.
