Targeted perturbations consist of controlled ablations or additive noise applied to these singular vector directions in the forward pass, calibrated to match the perturbation magnitude of entire heads, MLP blocks, or random neuron subsets, with careful consideration of layer norms and residual connections to isolate causal effects.

EXPERIMENT:  
We quantitatively evaluate the impact of perturbations by measuring perplexity increases and next-token prediction accuracy drops on held-out PTB and WikiText-103 datasets. Baselines include ablations of full attention heads, entire MLP blocks, and randomly selected neuron subsets of equivalent size and perturbation magnitude. Success is defined as observing at least a 15% larger degradation in perplexity and accuracy when perturbing identified sparse singular vector interactions compared to all baselines, thereby falsifying the null hypothesis that function resides predominantly at coarser component levels.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances mechanistic interpretability by operationalizing and validating sparse nonlinear interactions between singular vectors as primary functional units in transformers — a granularity beyond prior head- or block-level analyses and distinct from recent singular vector decompositions focused on isolated directions. Our approach introduces a concrete, algorithmic method to detect and causally test these interactions, rigorously controlling for perturbation scale and architectural confounds, thereby providing a novel mechanistic lens that bridges linear algebraic structure with nonlinear functional dynamics.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
Individual transformer attention heads implement partially distinct but overlapping subcircuits corresponding to specific linguistic phenomena, whose causal contributions can be precisely isolated and disrupted via combinatorial and fine-grained interventions, revealing the degree of modularity and linear separability in their computations.

BACKGROUND:  
Understanding whether attention heads in transformer language models act as independent, linguistically meaningful subcircuits remains an open challenge in mechanistic interpretability. Prior work has largely relied on correlational probes and single-head ablations, which struggle to disentangle overlapping or distributed representations and nonlinear interactions among heads. Precisely characterizing modularity and causal roles of heads is crucial for interpretability and model steering.

APPROACH:  
We propose a rigorous intervention-based framework combining linear and nonlinear probes, SVCCA, and alternative similarity metrics alongside causal mediation analysis and combinatorial ablations to test the modularity and linear separability of attention heads. Candidate heads linked to syntactic and semantic phenomena will be identified through supervised probes trained on attention patterns and neuron activations from transformer models such as GPT-2 Medium. Targeted interventions include fine-tuned learned masks, Gaussian noise injection, and coordinated multi-head perturbations to isolate causal effects while controlling for global degradation, head swapping, and perturbations to non-attention parameters.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate causal disruption effects using linguistically-motivated benchmarks—SyntaxGym (subject-verb agreement, garden path effects), SuperGLUE diagnostic sets (coreference, causal reasoning), and BLiMP (fine-grained syntactic contrasts)—measuring statistically significant selective drops in task performance (≥20% relative) after intervention on identified heads, minimal impact (≤5% relative) on unrelated tasks and general perplexity, and probe subspace similarity metrics (SVCCA below 0.3 and nonlinear alternatives). Controls include random and non-corresponding head ablations, head swaps, and perturbations outside attention modules.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work relying on correlational probing or simple ablation, this study integrates combinatorial and causal mediation methods to dissect overlapping and nonlinear interactions among attention heads, critically reevaluating assumptions of linear separability and modularity. It advances mechanistic interpretability by providing fine-grained causal evidence of linguistic subcircuits within transformer attention, with robust controls and richer metrics to distinguish true modularity from distributed computation.

topic: Mechanistic interpretability of transformer models
text:
IDEA:  
The majority of emergent linguistic features in transformer language models are encoded in stable, distributed linear subspaces spanning multiple attention heads and layers, rather than localized within individual heads, detectable via rigorously validated cross-layer canonical correlation analysis combined with probing and causal interventions.

BACKGROUND:  
Despite advances in mechanistic interpretability, it remains unclear whether fine-grained linguistic knowledge in transformers is localized to specific attention heads or distributed across stable subspaces spanning multiple heads and layers. Existing interpretability efforts often assume feature localization or treat attention heads as primary units of analysis, yet recent evidence suggests richer, more distributed encoding schemes that challenge this paradigm. Clarifying the spatial and representational structure of linguistic features is crucial for developing accurate mechanistic models and effective interpretability tools.

APPROACH:  
We propose a standardized, stable procedure to extract feature-specific activation subspaces by training supervised linear probes with consistent regularization and dimensionality controls across transformer layers and heads. We then apply cross-layer canonical correlation analysis (CCA) to quantitatively compare these multi-head subspaces against single-head subspaces, complemented by rigorous positive and negative controls using synthetic models with known localized or distributed features. To deepen interpretability, we incorporate causal interventions (e.g., activation ablations) and expand analyses to include MLP activations and residual streams, assessing nonlinear and dynamic encoding mechanisms beyond linear probes.

EXPERIMENT:  
Using the linguistically targeted BLiMP and MSGS datasets, we probe syntactic, morphological, semantic, and pragmatic features in multiple transformer models (GPT-2 small/medium and RoBERTa-base). Success metrics include maximum canonical correlations between multi-head and single-head subspaces, probe accuracy comparisons, and causal intervention effects. We require that for >70% of tested features, multi-head subspaces exhibit low canonical correlations (<0.5) with any single head and that single-head probe accuracy remains near chance or at least 10% lower than multi-head probes, thereby falsifying localization hypotheses. Baselines include random subspace controls and prior single-head probing methods.

NOVELTY:  
This work rigorously formalizes and validates a stable, reproducible methodology for subspace extraction and CCA in mechanistic interpretability, introduces causal interventions to link representational geometry with functional model behavior, and situates distributed feature encoding within a broader circuit-level framework—advancing beyond prior work that focuses mainly on individual components or singular vector decompositions without comprehensive validation or cross-layer integration.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporating explicit causal structure learning into reinforcement learning agents improves zero-shot generalization to unseen tasks exhibiting novel but causally related environment dynamics.

BACKGROUND:  
Zero-shot generalization in RL—ability to perform well on unseen tasks without additional training—remains challenging, especially when test environments vary in underlying causal factors such as gravity, friction, or object mass. While recent work leverages latent context inference or modular policies to capture task variation, these methods do not explicitly model causal relations in environment dynamics, limiting their ability to generalize systematically across causally related but structurally novel tasks.

APPROACH:  
We augment standard model-free RL agents (e.g., PPO or SAC) with a differentiable causal structure learning module inspired by Neural Relational Inference and continuous causal discovery frameworks. This module infers latent causal graphs from observed environment transitions and conditions the policy on these inferred structures via a modular architecture, jointly training causal graph encoders and policies end-to-end using reconstruction losses and policy gradients. Critical controls include dynamics models without causal structure, random graph encoders, and state-of-the-art latent context baselines to isolate the causal inductive bias effect.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate zero-shot generalization on systematically varied task distributions from the Procgen Benchmark and DeepMind Control Suite with Variable Dynamics, where training tasks explore known causal parameter variations and zero-shot test tasks contain novel causal parameter combinations. Performance is measured by normalized average episodic return on unseen tasks, with statistical significance assessed via multiple seeds and hypothesis correction; success requires ≥10% improvement over all baselines, while failing to surpass baselines by at least 5% falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior latent context inference or modular RL methods, our approach explicitly learns and leverages structured causal relations in environment dynamics rather than implicit context embeddings, providing a theoretically grounded inductive bias tailored for causal generalization; moreover, we rigorously validate causal graph quality and disentanglement in high-dimensional RL settings, establishing clear empirical evidence for or against the causal structural advantage in zero-shot task transfer.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Integrating explicit causal structure learning into policy representations enables improved zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning by disentangling latent causal factors beyond what context embedding methods achieve.

BACKGROUND: Zero-shot generalization (ZSG) in RL is challenged by hidden, latent causal confounders that alter environment dynamics in ways not captured by standard state observations or context embeddings. While recent work shows context embeddings can partially capture environment variations, they often fail to disentangle underlying causal mechanisms, limiting generalization to unseen settings where spurious correlations break down.

APPROACH: We propose an end-to-end RL framework that jointly learns a differentiable causal graph over latent state variables using a continuous-parameter causal discovery module (e.g., NOTEARS), integrating the learned adjacency matrix to produce causally disentangled latent representations for policy learning. This contrasts with context embedding baselines that concatenate learned environment parameters without causal structure modeling. We formalize assumptions for causal identifiability under sequential decision processes and connect low Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) in graph recovery to provable bounds on zero-shot performance degradation.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on CausalWorld robotic manipulation tasks and Procgen benchmark variants enriched with hidden causal confounders, measuring zero-shot normalized cumulative reward on held-out causal parameterizations and causal discovery quality via SHD against ground-truth graphs. Ablations include fixed/random causal graphs and equal-capacity embeddings without causal constraints, with baselines PPO, PERM, PPPO, and state-of-the-art context embedding methods. Failure to improve zero-shot reward by >5% or achieve SHD <10 falsifies the hypothesis.

NOVELTY: This work operationalizes causal discovery as an explicit inductive bias within deep RL policy learning rather than relying solely on behavior-specific context inference, providing formal theoretical grounding linking causal graph recovery to generalization guarantees, and demonstrating empirical causal disentanglement benefits on complex, high-dimensional benchmarks with latent confounders.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA:  
Integrating an explicit online causal discovery and inference module within policy networks enables superior zero-shot generalization to unseen environments exhibiting structural dynamic shifts, outperforming context representation learning alone.

BACKGROUND:  
Zero-shot generalization (ZSG) in reinforcement learning remains a critical challenge, especially when policies face unseen environment variants with fundamentally different causal structures or dynamics. Existing methods primarily leverage context representation learning or pessimistic policy evaluation, but they often fail to explicitly model and exploit underlying causal relationships that govern environment transitions, limiting their adaptability to structural shifts.

APPROACH:  
We propose augmenting a PPO-based policy network with a differentiable causal module that performs online causal graph discovery over observed state features and infers causal interventions corresponding to actions, enabling the policy to condition decisions on inferred causal variables rather than raw observations alone. This module operates under explicit assumptions—such as causal sufficiency over a latent low-dimensional representation and structural invariance of causal mechanisms across environment variants—to ensure identifiability and transferability. We contrast this approach against context-aware policies incorporating learned embeddings of environment-specific factors, as well as strong baselines like vanilla PPO and PERM.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate zero-shot generalization performance on the Procgen Benchmark (Cavefly and Jumper) and OpenAI Gym’s CarRacing-v2 with systematically perturbed physics parameters (e.g., friction, steering dynamics). Metrics include zero-shot task success rate, cumulative reward, and robustness measured by performance variance across >5 unseen environment variants. Statistical significance tests will compare the causal module policy to context-aware and capacity-matched ablation baselines to rigorously attribute improvements to causal inference.

NOVELTY:  
This work introduces a scalable, online causal discovery and inference layer tailored for high-dimensional RL environments with partial observability, explicitly formalizes assumptions enabling causal identifiability and transferability for zero-shot policy adaptation, and empirically disentangles causal inference from context representation learning—advancing beyond prior works that rely solely on implicit context embeddings or pessimistic policy evaluation without structural causal reasoning.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Integrating a two-level hierarchical latent variable model that separately encodes task-agnostic environmental dynamics and abstract reward-conditioned task representations can significantly improve zero-shot generalization across tasks with shared dynamics but structurally varying reward functions.

BACKGROUND: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning remains an open challenge, particularly when tasks share underlying environment dynamics but differ in reward structures and objectives. Existing context-based or modular methods often entangle dynamics and reward signals, limiting their ability to transfer effectively to novel tasks without fine-tuning. Decomposing the latent space hierarchically to isolate environment dynamics from reward-conditioned abstractions offers a principled way to leverage structural task similarities for improved generalization.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical latent variable model (HLVM) with two latent levels: a lower-level latent encoding task-agnostic environment dynamics via unsupervised predictive learning across all tasks, and a higher-level latent capturing abstract, reward-conditioned task features learned by conditioning on observed reward signals and task descriptors. A shared policy, trained with PPO, is conditioned jointly on both latents to enable effective zero-shot transfer to new tasks. At test time, latent variables are inferred without additional fine-tuning, enabling direct zero-shot deployment on unseen tasks with novel reward functions but familiar dynamics.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on carefully split subsets of Meta-World (MT10) and Procgen benchmarks, ensuring zero-shot evaluation on held-out tasks that share environment dynamics but differ in reward functions. Metrics include average normalized returns over 20 episodes per unseen task, comparing HLVM against (1) the 2024 “Inferring Behavior-Specific Context” model, (2) the 2025 MIGSS modular MARL baseline, (3) a non-hierarchical latent variable model jointly conditioned on dynamics and rewards, and (4) ablations removing either hierarchy or reward conditioning. Success is defined as at least a 10% improvement in normalized return over the strongest baseline, with failure declared if HLVM’s performance is within ±2% of baselines, or if ablations perform comparably.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior context or modular approaches, HLVM explicitly factorizes latent representations into dynamics and reward-conditioned components, enhancing identifiability and transfer by disentangling environment and task-specific variation. This principled hierarchical decomposition addresses limitations of entangled latent models and provides robustness to sparse or noisy reward signals, advancing theoretical understanding and empirical performance in zero-shot RL generalization.

topic: Zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning
text:
IDEA: Incorporating an explicitly trained counterfactual dynamics model into policy learning enhances zero-shot generalization in reinforcement learning beyond latent context inference alone by enabling causal reasoning about unseen environment variations.

BACKGROUND: Zero-shot generalization (ZSG) in reinforcement learning remains a critical challenge, particularly when agents encounter environment dynamics outside the training distribution. Prior work has focused on inferring latent context variables to adapt policies, but these approaches lack explicit causal reasoning about how dynamics changes affect state transitions, limiting robustness to unseen scenarios.

APPROACH: We extend standard RL architectures (e.g., PPO or SAC) augmented with learned context encoders by integrating a variational counterfactual dynamics module that predicts next states under hypothetical alterations of environment parameters (e.g., gravity, friction). This module is trained jointly with the policy to condition action selection not only on inferred context but also on counterfactual predictions, encouraging policies that generalize by reasoning about causal effects of dynamics changes rather than relying solely on contextual cues.

EXPERIMENT: We evaluate on Procgen Maze and Jumper environments alongside the DeepMind Control Suite with systematically varied physics parameters to create zero-shot test sets with dynamics beyond training ranges (e.g., ±20-30% changes in gravity and mass). Success is measured by at least 15% improvement in mean episodic return over a strong context-only baseline (Smith et al. 2024) across 20 random seeds, with capacity-matched non-counterfactual baselines included to control for model complexity. Ablation testing disables counterfactual reasoning at test time to verify its causal contribution. Statistical significance is assessed via paired t-tests (p < 0.05).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior context inference methods and model-based RL approaches, our method explicitly models and incorporates counterfactual transitions to enable reasoning over alternative dynamics distributions, grounded in a causal framework that isolates the effect of hypothetical interventions on environment parameters. This approach bridges latent context representation and causal counterfactual modeling, providing a novel mechanism for robust zero-shot policy generalization.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Applying persistent homology to time-varying high-dimensional datasets enables more accurate and robust detection of dynamic phase transitions in complex systems than temporally-aware clustering stability metrics and Mapper-based methods.

BACKGROUND:  
Detecting dynamic phase transitions in high-dimensional, time-dependent data remains challenging due to noise, annotation uncertainty, and limited temporal modeling in conventional clustering stability analyses. Existing topological approaches like Mapper partially capture data shape but do not explicitly quantify temporal evolution of topological features linked to phase changes. A rigorous, statistically principled framework that leverages persistent homology’s multi-scale, temporal topological summaries could improve phase transition detection and interpretability in synthetic and biological datasets.

APPROACH:  
We propose constructing Vietoris-Rips filtrations on high-dimensional point clouds at each discrete time point, computing persistence diagrams (dimensions 0 and 1), and tracking persistence landscapes and bottleneck distances over time to identify significant topological shifts indicative of phase transitions. Transitions will be detected via statistically controlled thresholding informed by permutation tests and bootstrap confidence intervals, explicitly accounting for temporal autocorrelation and annotation uncertainty. Parameter sensitivity analyses and robustness checks under varying noise levels will guide methodological rigor and interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
The method will be evaluated on Kuramoto model simulations with known synchronization transition points and single-cell RNA-seq time course data (GSE121654) annotated with pseudo-time and differentiation stage uncertainties. Baselines include temporally smoothed clustering stability metrics (average silhouette width, consensus clustering, gap statistic) applied with dynamic clustering/time-series models, and the Mapper algorithm’s graph connectivity changes. Detection accuracy will be quantified by precision, recall, and F1-score within ±5% time windows around ground truth or validated biological transitions, and robustness assessed via noise perturbation experiments. Statistical significance of performance differences will be assessed by paired tests across datasets and noise levels.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances beyond prior topological data analyses by integrating rigorous temporal modeling and statistical inference into persistent homology-based phase transition detection, explicitly addressing annotation uncertainty and parameter sensitivity while benchmarking against temporally-aware clustering baselines and Mapper. It provides a theoretically grounded, interpretable, and noise-robust framework to identify dynamic transitions in complex high-dimensional temporal data.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Incorporating persistent cohomology computations on the nerve simplicial complex derived from Mapper covers enables robust detection of higher-order topological features in high-dimensional data that are missed by standard Mapper and Ball Mapper.

BACKGROUND: Standard Mapper algorithms summarize data topology primarily through graph structures, which often fail to capture higher-dimensional features such as loops and voids critical for understanding complex data geometries. Existing approaches lack rigorous integration of persistent cohomology on Mapper nerves, limiting detection of multi-scale, higher-order relationships in both synthetic and real biological datasets with known intricate topology.

APPROACH: We propose enhancing the Mapper pipeline by computing persistent 1- and 2-dimensional cohomology groups directly on the nerve simplicial complex formed by Mapper covers, leveraging libraries such as GUDHI or Ripser for efficient computation. This approach complements the standard graph-based summaries by revealing additional topological invariants, which are quantitatively compared to ground truth Betti numbers on synthetic benchmarks and biologically validated branching features in single-cell RNA-seq data.

EXPERIMENT: Using the Swiss Roll with a hole and 3D Torus synthetic datasets with established Betti numbers as well as the human hematopoietic stem cell (GSE75478) single-cell RNA-seq dataset, we evaluate topology recovery accuracy via Jaccard similarity (≥0.8 threshold for success) against ground truth and measure biological interpretability through ≥5% improvement in clustering purity or silhouette scores with cohomology-informed features. Baselines include standard Mapper (Singh et al., 2007) and Ball Mapper.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior Mapper-based analyses that rely solely on graph summaries or clustering heuristics, our method uniquely integrates persistent cohomology on Mapper nerves with formal stability considerations and multi-dimensional feature detection, providing theoretical justification and empirical validation for uncovering higher-order topological structures beyond existing methods.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Incorporate persistent homology computed on deep neural network latent embeddings of high-dimensional data to reveal hierarchical topological features that are undetectable by Mapper or Ball Mapper applied directly to raw or dimensionally reduced data.

BACKGROUND:  
Topological Data Analysis (TDA) tools like Mapper and Ball Mapper have been successful in capturing coarse topological summaries of complex high-dimensional datasets, but often miss hierarchical or subtle topological features due to reliance on direct metric spaces or partial clustering techniques. Meanwhile, deep neural network embeddings, particularly those from Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) or contrastive learning models, can learn geometry-preserving latent representations that potentially enhance topological signal extraction. However, it remains an open problem whether combining persistent homology with learned embeddings unveils richer and more stable topological structures than current TDA methods alone.

APPROACH:  
We propose first embedding high-dimensional data into a lower-dimensional latent space using a VAE or contrastive learning-based encoder explicitly trained to preserve intrinsic data geometry under Lipschitz continuity assumptions. Persistent homology will then be computed on these learned latent point clouds using scalable algorithms (e.g., Ripser, GUDHI) with strategies such as subsampling and latent dimensionality capped at 10–20 for computational feasibility. We will compare the resulting persistence diagrams and barcode summaries against Mapper and Ball Mapper results applied on raw and PCA-reduced data to assess enhanced topological complexity and interpretability.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on the MNIST dataset (70,000 samples, 784 dims) and the Human Cell Atlas single-cell RNA-seq dataset (~100,000 cells, ~20,000 genes). Quantitative metrics include (a) the number and persistence of homological features (H0, H1, H2) above rigorously defined noise thresholds, and (b) semantic alignment measured by clustering purity, adjusted rand index (ARI), and normalized mutual information (NMI) with ground truth labels (digit classes for MNIST; cell differentiation stages for HCA). Statistical significance testing (p < 0.01) and ablation controls with random embeddings will determine the robustness and added value of the embedding step.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior TDA studies that apply Mapper or Ball Mapper directly on raw or linearly reduced data, this work systematically integrates theoretically grounded deep latent space embeddings with persistent homology to uncover hierarchical topological features, combined with scalable computational strategies and rigorous evaluation against strong baselines and random controls—addressing gaps in embedding stability, interpretability, and topological fidelity in high-dimensional biomedical and image domains.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA: Integrating differentiable persistent homology as a topological loss within a variational autoencoder enhances the detection of rare subpopulations in high-dimensional single-cell RNA-seq data beyond what standard topological data analysis or deep generative models achieve alone.

BACKGROUND: Rare cell subpopulations in single-cell transcriptomics are biologically critical but difficult to detect due to their subtle and complex high-dimensional signatures that are often lost in purely geometric embeddings or conventional clustering. While topological data analysis (TDA) methods like Mapper capture global shape information, and deep generative models such as scVI learn latent representations, neither fully exploits topological features during embedding learning to enhance rare population detection. This gap motivates developing an end-to-end approach that explicitly preserves topological structures characteristic of rare cells.

APPROACH: We propose a deep generative framework that incorporates a differentiable approximation of persistent homology computed on latent embeddings as a topological loss term combined with reconstruction and KL divergence losses in a variational autoencoder. This loss encourages the model to retain high-dimensional topological features (e.g., loops and voids) corresponding to rare subpopulations during training. Post-training, latent embeddings are clustered with HDBSCAN, and clusters enriched for rare cell markers are identified, thereby improving sensitivity and specificity for rare cell detection.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark this method on publicly available, well-annotated single-cell RNA-seq datasets containing known rare cell types: *Tabula Muris Senis* (Nature, 2020) and the 10x Genomics *PBMC 68k* dataset. Performance will be quantitatively assessed based on precision, recall, and F1-score for rare subpopulations against baselines including (1) Mapper and Ball Mapper combined with Louvain clustering, (2) scVI without topological loss, and (3) prior topologically regularized autoencoders if available. Ablation studies, negative controls (randomized topological loss), and bootstrap tests will rigorously evaluate statistical significance and cluster robustness.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior TDA applications that analyze data post hoc or deep generative models that ignore topology, this approach embeds a differentiable persistent homology loss directly into the latent space training objective, explicitly preserving topological signatures of rare cell types. This integration is uniquely tailored to rare subpopulation detection in high-dimensional biological data and is supported by a comprehensive evaluation framework including rigorous controls and scalable computational strategies.

topic: Topological methods for high-dimensional data analysis
text:
IDEA:  
Applying rigorously parameterized persistent homology to PHATE embeddings of time-series single-cell RNA-seq data can robustly capture dynamic topological features indicative of biological state transitions, complementing Mapper and Ball Mapper by revealing stable multi-scale loops and branches that correlate with annotated lineage progression.

BACKGROUND:  
Current topological data analysis (TDA) methods like Mapper and Ball Mapper effectively characterize cellular heterogeneity and continuous trajectories in high-dimensional single-cell data, but may miss certain persistent multiscale features linked to dynamic state transitions due to their clustering-centric design. Persistent homology (PH), when carefully parameterized and applied to dimensionally reduced embeddings, offers a principled way to detect connected components and loops that reflect branching and cyclic biological processes, yet its practical utility and robustness in this setting remain underexplored and theoretically subtle due to embedding nonlinearity and data noise.

APPROACH:  
We will preprocess scRNA-seq count data from hematopoiesis (Paul et al. 2015, GEO GSE72857) and MEF reprogramming (Schiebinger et al. 2019, GEO GSE110823), normalize and select highly variable genes, then apply PHATE for 2D/3D embeddings capturing continuous trajectories. Persistent homology will be computed via Vietoris-Rips filtrations on these embeddings, with parameters (e.g., scale thresholds) optimized through a bootstrap-based noise null model and automated stability metrics including bottleneck distance consistency and perturbation analyses. Persistent 0D and 1D features will be mapped back to known cell states and transitions and compared quantitatively against clusters and transition points identified by Mapper (using diffusion pseudotime filters) and Ball Mapper run under literature-recommended settings. Negative controls will include randomized embeddings and PH on original high-dimensional data to disentangle embedding artifacts. PH-based clustering will be defined via persistence-informed segmentation, benchmarked fairly against Mapper/Ball Mapper clusters.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate success by comparing Adjusted Rand Index (ARI) and Normalized Mutual Information (NMI) of clusters from PH-based methods versus Mapper and Ball Mapper against ground truth annotations, assessing sensitivity and specificity (>80% and >85% respectively) of detected topological features aligned with annotated branching/transition cells, and measuring computational efficiency and robustness across parameter perturbations. Failure is defined as PH underperforming by >10% in ARI/NMI or sensitivity <50%, or instability of persistent features under noise and parameter variation.

NOVELTY:  
This work advances beyond prior applications of Mapper and Ball Mapper by developing a rigorous, automated parameter tuning and validation framework for PH on nonlinear embeddings, explicitly addressing stability and noise robustness with theoretical grounding in PH stability theorems under metric perturbations. It provides the first systematic demonstration that PH can capture complementary dynamic topological features in biological time-series single-cell data, supported by comprehensive controls and quantitative benchmarks, thus clarifying when and how PH meaningfully augments existing TDA tools.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing mid-depth variational quantum circuits (8-12 layers) with noise-resilient angle encoding and randomized compiling will achieve a statistically significant generalization accuracy improvement of at least 3% over carefully matched classical baselines on high-dimensional, real-world datasets under limited training data conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Demonstrating a practical quantum advantage in machine learning remains an open challenge due to noise, limited circuit depth, and classical model competitiveness. Prior works have largely focused on asymptotic representational power or idealized settings, leaving unresolved whether noisy mid-depth variational quantum circuits (VQCs) with tailored noise-mitigation encoding can surpass classical models in realistic, high-dimensional supervised tasks with restricted data. This research addresses the gap by rigorously comparing quantum and classical models under identical noise and data constraints, focusing on generalization performance.

APPROACH:  
We propose training mid-depth hardware-efficient VQCs with approximately 8-12 layers of parameterized single-qubit rotations and CZ/CNOT gates, combined with noise-resilient input encoding via angle embedding augmented by randomized compiling to suppress coherent errors. Classical baselines will include a deep neural network (DNN) matched for effective model capacity through ablation studies, an XGBoost gradient-boosted tree ensemble, and an RBF kernel SVM, all optimized under identical limited training conditions. Noise effects will be realistically simulated using depolarizing and amplitude damping channels calibrated from current mid-scale quantum devices, and training will utilize parameter-shift gradient descent with measurement shot budgets and variance reduction techniques to ensure statistical robustness.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on two benchmark datasets representing diverse high-dimensional real-world tasks: Fashion-MNIST (28×28 grayscale images, 10 classes) and the UCI Higgs Boson dataset (28 features, binary classification). Models will be trained on 10% subsets of the training data with identical preprocessing (including PCA to ~20 features for quantum encoding), assessed over five independent 80/10/10 train/validation/test splits. The primary metric is test classification accuracy, with success defined as the quantum model achieving at least a 3% absolute improvement over all classical baselines with p < 0.05 (paired t-test). Secondary metrics include training runtime, noise robustness, and parameter efficiency.

NOVELTY:  
This work explicitly addresses practical feasibility by integrating noise-resilient encoding with randomized compiling and realistic noise models, quantifying quantum advantage under limited data and noise. It carefully matches classical baselines not only by parameter count but also by effective model capacity and inductive bias, thus improving upon prior studies that rely on idealized assumptions or coarse model comparisons. Furthermore, the adjusted performance threshold and dataset choice are informed by recent empirical and theoretical insights, pushing beyond the representability-focused literature to establish a concrete, reproducible benchmark for mid-depth VQC advantages in practical ML tasks.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing variational circuits with adaptively optimized entanglement patterns achieve lower generalization error than matched classical models on real-world, high-dimensional datasets under limited training samples, demonstrating practical quantum advantage in few-shot classification.

BACKGROUND:  
Current variational quantum machine learning (QML) approaches often use fixed entanglement structures, which may limit their expressivity or trainability, especially in realistic low-data regimes. Demonstrating a clear quantum advantage in generalization for practical, noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices remains an open problem, as existing studies either focus on idealized tasks or lack rigorous baseline comparisons and ablation analyses. Establishing whether adaptive entanglement architectures can overcome known VQC trainability challenges and yield tangible benefits on real-world data is critical to validating QML’s practical utility.

APPROACH:  
We propose a variational quantum circuit model that encodes input features via a hybrid strategy—using amplitude encoding for low-dimensional subsets where qubit resources permit, and angle encoding otherwise—followed by parameterized rotation layers interleaved with entangling gates whose connectivity is dynamically adapted via a sparsity-regularized objective and optimized with gradient-based heuristics coupled to a classical outer loop (e.g., evolutionary algorithm). This adaptive entanglement aims to tailor circuit expressivity and mitigate barren plateaus, leveraging entanglement measures tracked during training to guide structure updates. Classical baselines matched in parameter counts, including deep neural networks, SVMs with RBF kernels, and classical models simulating shallow quantum circuits, will be used to isolate the effect of adaptive entanglement on generalization.

EXPERIMENT:  
We benchmark on the Fashion-MNIST image classification dataset (28x28 grayscale, 10 classes) and the California Housing Prices dataset converted to a 3-class binning classification task, subsampling training sets at 10%, 20%, and 30% to emulate low-data conditions. Model performance is evaluated by average test error over 10 randomized splits, with statistical significance assessed via paired t-tests (p < 0.05). Controlled ablations compare adaptive entanglement to fixed entanglement patterns and no entanglement, ensuring differences arise from connectivity adaptation rather than parameter count or encoding scheme. Success is defined as a consistent ≥5% absolute test error reduction over all classical baselines across datasets and sample regimes; failure or marginal (<1%) improvements falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates adaptive entanglement structure optimization within VQCs guided by entanglement-aware regularization and gradient-informed search, rigorously isolating its impact on generalization in realistic low-data, high-dimensional tasks. Unlike prior studies focused on fixed architectures or theoretical representability, it empirically and statistically validates the practical advantage of entanglement adaptation against strong, capacity-matched classical baselines, advancing the scientific understanding of quantum architecture search in machine learning.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Hybrid quantum-classical machine learning models employing trainable adaptive quantum feature maps integrated with deep classical networks achieve statistically significant (>5% relative) reductions in generalization error compared to fully classical counterparts on high-dimensional real-world tasks, specifically CIFAR-100 image classification and TCGA Pan-Cancer gene expression subtype classification.

BACKGROUND:  
Demonstrating quantum advantage in practical machine learning remains an open challenge, particularly on real-world, high-dimensional datasets where classical models excel and synthetic benchmarks fail to capture complexity. Existing quantum ML work largely focuses on theoretical expressivity or synthetic data, leaving a gap in understanding whether adaptive quantum feature maps can translate into meaningful performance gains when combined with deep classical architectures on challenging tasks involving complex data correlations and dimensionality.

APPROACH:  
We propose jointly training parameterized quantum circuits as adaptive feature maps that encode dimension-reduced classical inputs into quantum states, integrated within classical deep networks (ResNet18 for CIFAR-100 and tailored MLPs for TCGA data). Dimensionality reduction via task-aware methods (e.g., PCA plus supervised feature selection) reduces input to a manageable number of qubits (~20–30), enabling realistic noisy quantum circuit simulations with modest depth (6 layers) and shot-limited measurements reflecting near-term hardware constraints. Hybrid gradient-based training optimizes quantum and classical parameters end-to-end, with noise models and regularization mitigating barren plateaus and overfitting.

EXPERIMENT:  
We conduct controlled experiments comparing hybrid QML models against (1) classical deep networks of matched architecture and parameter count, (2) classical kernel methods approximating the quantum kernel, and (3) classical models augmented with random Fourier features, across CIFAR-100 (60k images, 100 classes) and TCGA Pan-Cancer (10k samples, ~20k gene features). Primary evaluation metrics are test set classification error with ≥5% relative improvement threshold and statistical significance assessed via repeated cross-validation and bootstrap confidence. Secondary metrics include calibration error and adversarial robustness.

NOVELTY:  
This study rigorously addresses scalability and feasibility of adaptive quantum feature maps on truly high-dimensional real-world data through principled dimensionality reduction and noisy circuit simulation reflecting near-term devices, while enforcing fair and capacity-matched classical baselines with learned embeddings. It advances beyond prior theoretical and synthetic benchmark work by empirically validating quantum advantage claims in integrated hybrid models on complex, practical datasets, and grounding the approach in realistic training dynamics that mitigate optimization challenges like barren plateaus.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing mid-depth parametrized quantum circuits with rigorously implemented adaptive measurement schemes can achieve at least 10% lower generalization error than optimally tuned classical deep neural networks and Gaussian Process Regression on noisy nonlinear regression tasks.

BACKGROUND:  
Demonstrating practical quantum advantage in machine learning remains an open challenge, particularly on realistic noisy regression problems where classical models are known to perform strongly. Prior work has linked quantum circuit expressivity to function representability but has yet to conclusively show that mid-depth circuits combined with adaptive measurement protocols can overcome classical baselines in generalization on noisy, nonlinear data. Establishing such an advantage would clarify the potential and limitations of near-term quantum hardware in machine learning.

APPROACH:  
We propose encoding noisy nonlinear regression inputs into mid-depth (10-15 layers) hardware-efficient parametrized quantum circuits with angle embeddings, followed by adaptive measurement strategies that iteratively optimize measurement bases and shot allocations to minimize estimation variance. Training proceeds via gradient-based optimization using parameter-shift rules minimizing mean squared error, with careful error mitigation and noise modeling included to mirror near-term hardware conditions. Classical baselines include fully optimized deep neural networks (3-5 layers with dropout and batch normalization) and Gaussian Process Regression with RBF kernels, all rigorously hyperparameter-tuned to ensure capacity parity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Testing occurs on (1) the NOISE-Injected Friedman #1 synthetic regression dataset with controlled Gaussian noise levels (σ = 0.1, 0.3, 0.5) on features and targets, and (2) the QM9 quantum chemistry dataset predicting molecular energies inherently corrupted by experimental noise. The primary evaluation metric is held-out test mean squared error (MSE), with statistical significance assessed over at least five independent runs per noise level using cross-validation. Confirmed quantum advantage requires the QML model to consistently achieve ≥10% lower test MSE than both classical baselines across noise regimes; failure to meet this or convergence issues falsify the hypothesis.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely combines mid-depth PQCs with a theoretically and experimentally validated adaptive measurement protocol designed to reduce measurement noise and variance, addressing trainability and noise robustness challenges often overlooked in prior studies. It explicitly matches classical model capacities and employs controlled noise injection on well-understood benchmarks, grounding the quantum advantage claim in rigorous experimental feasibility and statistical analysis beyond existing purely theoretical or shallow-circuit demonstrations.

topic: Quantum advantage in machine learning tasks
text:
IDEA:  
Quantum machine learning models employing adaptive noise-aware training achieve statistically significant lower generalization error than state-of-the-art classical noise-robust algorithms with analogous adaptive noise handling on real-world noisy datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Robustness to noise is a critical challenge in applying machine learning to real-world data, where label and input corruption degrade model performance. While quantum machine learning (QML) offers theoretical advantages in representational power and noise resilience, it remains unclear if these advantages persist after accounting for noise-aware training techniques used classically. Establishing a fair, noise-adaptive comparative framework is essential to rigorously determine whether QML can practically surpass classical methods under realistic noisy conditions.

APPROACH:  
We implement a variational quantum circuit classifier trained on near-term quantum hardware and simulators using an adaptive noise-aware training protocol that iteratively estimates hardware noise parameters (e.g., depolarizing and dephasing rates) via noise tomography and incorporates these estimates into a noise-regularized cost function to improve generalization. To ensure an apples-to-apples comparison, classical baselines—including ResNet-50 with Generalized Cross Entropy loss, XGBoost with noise-injection and augmentation, and Co-teaching—are augmented with analogous adaptive noise parameter estimation and noise-aware optimization. The quantum circuit encodes data via amplitude or angle encoding, and training accounts for practical NISQ constraints such as limited qubit counts and coherence times.

EXPERIMENT:  
We evaluate all models on the Berlin Brain Connectivity Benchmark (BBCB) EEG dataset with real noisy artifacts and the CIFAR-10-C corrupted image dataset across multiple noise types and severity levels. The primary metric is classification generalization error on held-out noisy test sets, with quantum advantage defined as achieving at least a 5% absolute reduction in test error compared to the best classical noise-adaptive baseline, validated by paired bootstrap hypothesis testing (p < 0.05). Secondary metrics include runtime and quantum resource overhead to assess practical feasibility.

NOVELTY:  
This work uniquely integrates adaptive noise-aware training symmetrically across quantum and classical models to isolate genuine quantum advantage from mere noise-adaptive algorithmic improvements. It also rigorously addresses NISQ hardware limitations through realistic noise characterization protocols and scalable training strategies, providing the first comprehensive empirical and theoretical framework explicitly linking quantum noise models to improved robustness in noisy real-world classification tasks.
